Title: AI Diplomacy
Summary: Open-source project (GoodStartLabs, ~684 stars) where seven frontier LLMs (Claude, GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek, Llama, Qwen, Mistral, etc.) each control a power in the board game Diplomacy, negotiating, allying and betraying each other; transcripts were widely shared as public evidence of emergent LLM negotiation/deception behavior.
Importance/Impact: 684 GitHub stars, actively maintained; this negotiation-game project (frontier models play Diplomacy) drew substantial AI-community/media attention as one of the most-cited public demonstrations of multi-model negotiation, alliance-building, and betrayal behavior in LLMs.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is used for (1) giving several Diplomacy powers open-weight models (Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-235B, DeepSeek-Prover, Mistral-Small, Phi-4, Gemma-3, incl. ':free' tier variants) that compete head-to-head in the same game against proprietary models called via native OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini APIs; (2) as the default backend for the 'formatter' model (config.py) that converts each power's free-text LLM output into the structured JSON orders actually submitted to the game engine; (3) as the default backend for the narrative/phase-summary model; and (4) as the LLM judge in analyze_game_moments.py that auto-detects betrayals/lies/collaborations for highlight content.
Mistakes (null if safe): ai_diplomacy/clients.py:1186-1192 (OpenRouterClient.generate_response) sends only model/messages/max_tokens/temperature — no `provider` dict anywhere in the class — so open-weight powers (e.g. README.md:376 'ITALY': 'openrouter-meta-llama/llama-4-maverick') are load-balanced across providers/quantizations run-to-run (M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic routing). No require_parameters is set anywhere in the repo (grep confirms zero non-commented hits), so the temperature sampling param can be silently dropped by whichever backend is picked (M2). ai_diplomacy/utils.py:367-390 (log_llm_response) writes only {timestamp, model, power, phase, response_type, raw_input, raw_response, success} to CSV — the served provider and generation id are never recorded, so which weights actually produced any game's orders is unrecoverable after the fact (M4). Some defaults are bare/undated, e.g. analyze_game_moments.py:65 'openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview' with no snapshot date (M6). Most importantly, the project's entire premise is head-to-head comparison of named models' negotiation/betrayal behavior — README.md:370-380 assigns proprietary models (o3, claude-opus-4-20250514, gpt-4.1, gemini-2.5-pro-preview) and OpenRouter open-weight models (llama-4-maverick, gemini-2.5-flash-preview-05-20) to different powers in the *same* game. The proprietary legs run through pinned, single-provider native APIs while the open-weight legs run through completely unpinned OpenRouter — an asymmetric confound (M8) that directly threatens any 'model X betrayed/won more than model Y' claim drawn from the publicly shared transcripts. Nothing in README.md or the code discloses this routing/quantization risk to readers of the transcripts (M10); the sole sign the authors ever considered it is one commented-out provider pin for a single Qwen/Cerebras experiment (ai_diplomacy/clients.py:1361-1365, `"order": ["Cerebras"], "allow_fallbacks": False`) that was never enabled and never generalized to any other model or call site, including the formatter/judge calls that convert free text into actual game orders.
One-line fix: Set provider={"order"/"only": [...], "quantizations": [...], "require_parameters": true, "data_collection": "deny"} on every OpenRouterClient/RequestsOpenAIClient call (especially the default formatter/narrative models in config.py and every open-weight power model), and log the response's actual `provider` field + generation id into llm_responses.csv alongside model_name.

Title: AI Induced Psychosis: A shallow investigation (Tim Hua)
Summary: A red-teaming pipeline where one LLM roleplays an escalating psychotic user against a target model for N turns while a third LLM grades each target response on pushback/delusion-validation scales; results are aggregated into CSVs and plotted to compare psychosis-inducing tendencies across ~9 frontier/open-weight models for a widely-read Alignment Forum/LessWrong post.
Importance/Impact: Widely-discussed post on the 'AI psychosis' phenomenon (a topic that received significant mainstream media attention through 2025-2026); repo has 52 GitHub stars, well above typical single-post LW repos. Systematically red-teams and grades multiple frontier models (Grok, Gemini, Kimi, DeepSeek) for psychosis-inducing behavior, a novel/timely safety concern.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the inference backend (via inspect_ai's `get_model()`) for the majority of target models under test (grok-3, grok-4, gemini-2.5-flash/pro, kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1-0528, gpt-oss-20b) and for both the red-teaming "psychotic user" model and the LLM grader (default `openrouter/x-ai/grok-4` or `grok-3`), whose outputs are parsed into the numeric ratings (pushback score, delusion-confirmation score, therapeutic-collaboration scores, etc.) that drive every comparative chart in the post.
Mistakes (null if safe): Critical route: OpenRouter-served completions ARE the data -- target-model comparisons (pushback/delusion-validation charts) and the grader's numeric scores drive the post's headline model-ranking claims. M1/M3: kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1-0528 and gpt-oss-20b are all multi-provider on OpenRouter with materially different quantizations across backends, yet every call goes out with default probabilistic load-balanced routing and no quant floor -- model-to-model and run-to-run comparisons of "psychosis-inducing behavior" are confounded by whichever backend/quant served each turn. M4: no provider or generation-id is ever recorded (confirmed against real CSV columns and the ChatMessage serialization in save_conversation), so which backend produced any given rating is unrecoverable, threatening every reported comparison. M5: data_collection is never set to 'deny', so prompts (detailed psychotic-delusion roleplay) default to OpenRouter's 'allow' policy on whichever provider serves them. M6: several DEFAULT_MODELS slugs are bare/undated (kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, grok-3, grok-4, gemini-2.5-flash/pro), so a rerun later may silently hit a different checkpoint than the one graded in the post. M8: the post's central charts (per its own text) compare these unpinned open-weight/OpenRouter-routed models directly against models called via effectively single-served direct APIs (chatgpt-4o-latest/gpt-5 via OpenAI API, claude-sonnet-4 via Anthropic API), mixing fully-pinned and completely-unpinned inference paths under one "model performance" ranking. Partial credit on disclosure: the post does name OpenRouter as the backend and flags "I use the default API settings in the InspectAI package" as a caveat, clearing the bare M10 threshold of disclosing the inference stack -- but never connects this to quantization/provider-drift as a threat to the cross-model rankings, hence "aware_partial" not "aware_and_handled." M2/M7/M9/M11/M12 do not apply: no sampling params (temperature/top_p/seed) are set anywhere (grep confirmed empty), the grader parses free-text JSON via json.loads() rather than response_format/schema so nothing is silently dropped, and no :floor/:nitro price-sort suffixes are used. Fun/telling detail: README line 7 notes "Grok-4 on openrouter has started refusing to role play as a psychotic user. However, I found that Grok-3 is just as good and doesn't refuse" -- the author already observed behavior drift on an unpinned route and worked around it by swapping models rather than pinning the route, exactly the failure mode M1/M3/M6 predict.
One-line fix: Pass provider={"quantizations":["fp16","bf16","fp8"],"data_collection":"deny","sort":"exacto"} as a model_arg to inspect_ai's get_model() for every openrouter/... target/redteam/grader model, and log each response's OpenRouter 'provider' field / generation id alongside every saved transcript.

Title: ARC-AGI Benchmarking (ARC Prize Foundation)
Summary: Official evaluation harness for the ARC-AGI / ARC Prize benchmark, running candidate LLMs against ARC-AGI tasks via a pluggable set of provider adapters (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Fireworks, Grok, OpenRouter, MuleRouter, etc.) to produce pass/fail scores that feed the public ARC-AGI leaderboard.
Importance/Impact: Official evaluation harness for the ARC Prize / ARC-AGI benchmark created by François Chollet — arguably the most media-covered 'general reasoning' benchmark in AI (cited in OpenAI's o3 announcement, $1M+ prize purses, ARC-AGI-2 paper arXiv:2505.11831). 351 GitHub stars on the tooling repo itself; the benchmark's results are cited constantly in reasoning-model discourse.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouterAdapter (src/arc_agi_benchmarking/adapters/openrouter.py) wraps the OpenAI SDK pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 and is used as the serving path for 13 model configs in models.yml, including several open-weight, multi-provider models: DeepSeek-R1-0528, Qwen3-235B-A22B-07-25, Mistral's Magistral-small/medium-2506 (+thinking variant), plus proprietary models like grok-3/grok-3-mini-beta and gemini-2.5-pro-preview.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1/M3 (Unpinned quantization / probabilistic routing, HIGH): Of the 13 model configs in src/arc_agi_benchmarking/models.yml with provider: "openrouter", only 2 set extra_body.provider.only to pin a backend (claude-4-sonnet-...-bedrock -> only:[amazon-bedrock] at models.yml:1050-1052; gemini-2-5-pro-preview-openrouter-thinking-1k -> only:[google-ai-studio] at models.yml:1394-1396). The remaining 11 -- including the clearly open-weight, multi-provider models deepseek_r1_0528-openrouter (models.yml:1458-1465), qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25 (models.yml:1562-1569), magistral-small-2506/magistral-medium-2506/magistral-medium-2506-thinking (models.yml:1407-1433) -- have no provider order/only/sort and no quantizations field, so OpenRouter is free to load-balance each request across whichever upstream inference provider is available (Fireworks/Together/DeepInfra/Novita/etc.), each potentially serving a different quantization (down to int4/fp8). Because this harness generates the official ARC-AGI leaderboard scores for these exact models, a leaderboard score attributed to "DeepSeek R1" or "Qwen3-235B" may reflect a randomly-selected backend/precision rather than a fixed, reproducible configuration, and repeat runs can silently drift. M4 (No provenance logging, HIGH): AttemptMetadata.provider (src/arc_agi_benchmarking/schemas.py:128) is populated from the static config string self.model_config.provider (openai_base.py:102), i.e. always the literal string "openrouter" -- never the actual serving backend or generation id that OpenRouter's API returns per response. AttemptMetadata (schemas.py:122-131) has no field for served-provider or generation_id at all, and a repo-wide grep for generation_id/provider_used/x-openrouter/response.provider returned nothing. There is therefore no way, even after the fact, to audit which upstream provider/quantization actually produced any given official ARC-AGI attempt for an OpenRouter-routed model. M6 (Model version drift, low/medium): grok-3-openrouter maps to model_name: "x-ai/grok-beta" (models.yml:810), a bare undated alias slug rather than a dated snapshot, so the served weights can change silently between evaluation runs. M8 (Cross-provider comparison confound, HIGH given this is a comparative leaderboard): The leaderboard juxtaposes proprietary models served through single, consistent direct APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini native adapters) against open-weight models served unpinned through OpenRouter's multi-provider pool -- an asymmetric, uncontrolled source of score variance baked into head-to-head leaderboard comparisons. Note the team clearly knows the extra_body.provider.only mechanism exists (used it twice) but did not apply it to the higher-risk open-weight models where multi-provider quantization variance on OpenRouter is best documented (DeepSeek R1, Qwen3, Magistral) -- hence 'aware_partial' rather than 'unaware'. M2 (silent parameter dropping) and M9 (judge on unconstrained route) were checked and do not apply: none of the 13 OpenRouter configs set temperature/top_p/response_format, and a full-repo grep for response_format/json_schema/require_parameters/data_collection/quantiz/extra_body found no require_parameters or data_collection usage anywhere, but also no reliance on strict sampling or structured-output params that would silently break.
Safeguards present: extra_body.provider.only (used on only 2 of 13 openrouter configs)
One-line fix: Add extra_body.provider.only (or order + quantizations) pinning to every OpenRouter model config -- especially the open-weight ones (DeepSeek R1, Qwen3-235B, Magistral) -- and add a served_provider / generation_id field to AttemptMetadata populated from the OpenRouter response so every leaderboard attempt is auditable post hoc.
Venue: arXiv / ARC Prize official leaderboard tooling

Title: AgentLab (ServiceNow Research)
Summary: AgentLab is ServiceNow Research's open-source framework for building and benchmarking LLM web agents (GenericAgent, WebArena/VisualWebArena agents, etc.) against BrowserGym-based benchmarks (WebArena, VisualWebArena, WorkArena, MiniWoB), feeding an official public leaderboard and the BrowserGym Ecosystem TMLR paper.
Importance/Impact: 606 GitHub stars; official open-source agent framework from ServiceNow Research (authors of WorkArena/BrowserGym) used to run and benchmark web agents on WebArena, VisualWebArena, WorkArena, and MiniWoB.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is a first-class, directly-instantiated LLM backend for agent rollouts. `OpenRouterModelArgs` (src/agentlab/llm/chat_api.py:79-89, and a second copy in src/agentlab/llm/response_api.py:966-982) builds an `OpenAI`-SDK client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 and every action-generating LLM call during a benchmark episode goes straight through it. Named, ready-to-run agent configs use it directly, e.g. WA_AGENT_SONNET / VWA_AGENT_SONNET in src/agentlab/agents/visualwebarena/agent.py:299-317 (`CHAT_MODEL_ARGS_DICT["openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet:beta"]`), and multiple open-weight configs in src/agentlab/agents/generic_agent/agent_configs.py and hint_use_agent/agent_configs.py using `openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct`, `llama-3-70b-instruct`, `llama-3.1-70b-instruct`, `llama-4-maverick`, plus `openrouter/openai/o3-mini`, `o1-mini-2024-09-12`. The full model catalog (src/agentlab/llm/llm_configs.py:256-377) also defines `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1`, `qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct`, `openai/gpt-oss-120b`, `openai/gpt-oss-20b`. The agent's chosen action on every benchmark step is parsed directly from this call's output, so it is a critical route to the reported task-success-rate numbers.
Mistakes (null if safe): The actual API call site (`ChatModel.__call__`, src/agentlab/llm/chat_api.py:290-309: `self.client.chat.completions.create(model=self.model_name, messages=messages, n=n_samples, temperature=temperature, max_completion_tokens=self.max_tokens, logprobs=self.log_probs)`) never sets an `extra_body`/`provider` payload — confirmed by grep across the entire repo (`extra_body|require_parameters|data_collection|quantizations|"order"|allow_fallbacks`) returning zero hits in src/, tutorials/, tests/. The parallel path in src/agentlab/llm/response_api.py:620-651 (`OpenAIChatCompletionModel._call_api`) is identical: no provider config, and it additionally passes `tool_choice`/forced-tool-call params (lines 638-647) used to extract the agent's environment action from tool_calls — an unconstrained-route risk analogous to M9 (forced structured output with no require_parameters) since a provider that silently ignores tool_choice would corrupt the extracted action. Concretely: (1) M1 — open-weight models (llama-3.1-405b/70b/8b, llama-3-70b, llama-4-maverick, deepseek-r1, qwen-2-72b, gpt-oss-120b/20b) can be served at any quantization OpenRouter chooses on a given call, directly moving WebArena/VisualWebArena/MiniWoB task-success rates for those named agents. (2) M2 — temperature/n_samples are passed with no require_parameters, so a provider could silently ignore temperature=0.1/0.0 (reproducibility mode sets temperature to 0.0 in VisualWebArenaAgentArgs.set_reproducibility_mode) without AgentLab knowing. (3) M3 — no order/only/sort anywhere means every one of these calls is default load-balanced; a single benchmark run of hundreds of episodes can silently mix multiple backend providers for the same named agent, adding run-to-run and even within-run variance to the reported success rate. (4) M4 — `LLMTracker`/`Stats` (src/agentlab/llm/tracking.py:35-56, 91-95) only ever record input_tokens/output_tokens/cost; grep for `.provider`, `generation_id`, `completion.id` across src/agentlab/llm/*.py shows no field of the served provider or OpenRouter generation id is ever captured or stored with results, so a given episode's outcome cannot be traced to which backend produced it. (5) M5 — data_collection is never restricted, so prompts (including live web page content/screenshots for VWA) may be sent to data-collecting sub-providers by default. (6) M6 — several open-weight slugs are bare/undated (`meta-llama/llama-4-maverick`, `deepseek/deepseek-r1`, `qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct`) in contrast to the dated slugs used for the direct-OpenAI configs in the same dict (e.g. `gpt-4.1-2025-04-14`), so the exact checkpoint behind results isn't pinned. (7) M8 — this is a comparison/leaderboard framework: the same table of agents mixes proprietary single-served models called directly (OpenAIModelArgs/AzureModelArgs/AnthropicModelArgs/BedrockModelArgs — low risk) with open-weight models called only via unpinned OpenRouter — any 'model X beats model Y on WebArena' conclusion drawn across this mixed backend set is confounded by unmeasured provider/quantization variance on the OpenRouter side. (8) M10 — README.md has a detailed, good-faith '↻ Reproducibility' section (lines 248-289) enumerating software-version drift, API models silently changing, live-website drift, temperature, and non-deterministic tasks as reproducibility risks, and even ships a `reproducibility_journal.csv` + `ReproducibilityAgent` — but never once mentions OpenRouter provider routing, quantization, or which inference backend actually served a call, despite `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` being documented as a first-class env var (README.md:287) right next to it. That section is exactly where this should have been disclosed and was not.
One-line fix: In OpenRouterChatModel.__call__ (chat_api.py) and OpenAIChatCompletionModel._call_api (response_api.py), pass extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "require_parameters": True, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}} on every chat.completions.create call, and store the response's provider/generation id in AgentInfo/stats alongside each episode's result.
Venue: other (framework backing 2024 TMLR submission "The BrowserGym Ecosystem for Web Agent Research"; public HuggingFace leaderboard)

Title: Aider (AI pair-programming CLI + polyglot code benchmark / leaderboard)
Summary: Aider is a popular (47k+ star) open-source AI pair-programming CLI that supports many LLM backends via litellm, including OpenRouter. It also ships a "polyglot" coding benchmark (benchmark/benchmark.py) whose results populate a widely-cited public leaderboard comparing coding ability across frontier and open-weight models.
Importance/Impact: 47,473 GitHub stars — one of the most popular open-source AI coding tools. Its benchmark/leaderboard is a de facto community reference point for 'how good is model X at code,' regularly cited in blog posts and model release comparisons.
What they use OpenRouter for: Two uses: (1) end-user CLI tool — any user can point aider at `openrouter/<provider>/<model>`; (2) the polyglot benchmark harness (benchmark/benchmark.py) instantiates the exact same `aider.models.Model` -> `litellm.completion` code path to run 225 coding exercises per model and record pass rates that are published on the public Aider leaderboard. At least 11 of the ~151 published leaderboard rows were run with `aider --model openrouter/...` (e.g. openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2, openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b, openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b, openrouter/google/gemma-3-27b-it, openrouter/all-hands/openhands-lm-32b-v0.1, openrouter/x-ai/grok-4, openrouter/x-ai/grok-3-beta, openrouter/openai/o1, plus stealth models openrouter/openrouter/quasar-alpha and openrouter/openrouter/optimus-alpha), several of which are open-weight models.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization) and M3 (probabilistic provider routing): none of the OpenRouter-run leaderboard rows set `quantizations` or `order`/`only`/`sort`, so open-weight entries (kimi-k2, qwen3-32b, gpt-oss-120b, gemma-3-27b-it, openhands-lm-32b-v0.1) could have been served at any quantization by whichever provider won that call's load-balancing draw — directly threatens the reported pass_rate_1/pass_rate_2 numbers for those specific leaderboard rows, since backend/quantization choice alone can move code-benchmark accuracy by double digits. M4 (no provenance logging): benchmark.py's results dict never records the serving provider or generation id, so a later reader cannot verify or reproduce which backend actually produced a given leaderboard row's score. M6 (model version drift): several OpenRouter slugs used on the leaderboard are bare/undated (e.g. `openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2`, `openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b`), so the underlying weights behind that slug can change server-side after the leaderboard entry was published, silently invalidating the recorded score as a description of "the model" going forward. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the single ranked polyglot leaderboard table directly compares OpenRouter-routed, unpinned open-weight models against direct-API proprietary models (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/xAI called directly) — any performance gap involving an OpenRouter-routed row is partly confounded by unverified provider/quantization rather than being solely a capability difference, undermining the leaderboard's core comparative claim. M10 (no reporting): the canonical methodology post for this benchmark (2024-12-21-polyglot.md) never discusses OpenRouter routing risk, quantization, or inference-stack provenance, despite ~7% of published rows depending on it.
One-line fix: For leaderboard-producing runs, pin OpenRouter routing per model-settings entry (extra_params.extra_body.provider: {order/only, quantizations: ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], require_parameters: true, data_collection: "deny", allow_fallbacks: false}) and extend benchmark.py's results dict to capture and persist the response's actual serving `provider` field (or GET /api/v1/generation?id=...) alongside each row.
Venue: Community/industry — 47,473+ GitHub stars; leaderboard is a de facto reference cited in model-release comparisons

Title: AppWorld
Summary: AppWorld is an interactive coding/function-calling agent benchmark (750 tasks over 9 apps/457 APIs) from Stony Brook NLP with an official public leaderboard; it ships agent implementations (function-calling agent, ReAct code agent) plus a HAL-leaderboard export pipeline that turns agent rollout logs into published, comparable scores.
Importance/Impact: 461 GitHub stars; ACL 2024 Best Resource Paper award; widely-used interactive coding/function-calling agent benchmark (750 tasks over 9 apps/457 APIs) from Stony Brook NLP, with an official leaderboard.
What they use OpenRouter for: Production leaderboard-submission agent rollouts: `experiments/configs/{simplified_function_calling_agent,simplified_react_code_agent}/*/*_openrouter/{test_normal,test_challenge}.jsonnet` run the actual leaderboard test splits for four open-weight models (deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b, gpt-oss-20b) exclusively through OpenRouter via the litellm client (`"client_name": "litellm"`, `"name": "openrouter/<org>/<model>"`). Output flows straight into `scripts/appworld_to_hal_leaderboard.py`, which republishes the same logs to the HAL leaderboard.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization, High): deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b are all open-weight and called via a bare "openrouter/…" slug with no `quantizations` filter, so each leaderboard run may silently be served at a different precision (int4→fp32) run-to-run, threatening every reported score for these four models on the public AppWorld/HAL leaderboard. M2 (silent parameter dropping, High): temperature=0, seed=100, tool_choice="auto", parallel_tool_calls=true, reasoning_effort are all sent with no `require_parameters`, so a route that doesn't honor one can silently ignore it rather than error — corrupting the function-calling agent's behavior without any visible failure. M3 (probabilistic provider routing, High): no order/only/sort anywhere, so each of the many task-level calls (750 tasks × up to 50 steps) load-balances across whichever backend OpenRouter picks that instant — different tasks within the same reported run, and repeat runs across time, can hit different providers. M4 (no provenance logging, High): neither lm_calls.jsonl nor the HAL leaderboard export records which provider/quantization actually served each call, so a published score can never be traced back to what was actually run, let alone reproduced. M5 (data-policy leakage, Med): `data_collection` is never set (defaults to "allow"), so AppWorld task prompts/tool schemas may be retained/trained-on by whichever third-party backend gets routed to. M6 (model version drift, Med): llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b are bare undated slugs (deepseek-r1-0528 at least has a date suffix), so the served checkpoint isn't guaranteed stable across leaderboard resubmissions. M7 (seed→determinism assumption, Med): seed=100 is passed as if it yields reproducible generations, but combined with M1/M3 (unpinned quantization/provider) this gives no real determinism guarantee for the leaderboard numbers. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound, High): the AppWorld leaderboard directly compares these unpinned, OpenRouter-routed open-weight models against each other and against single-served, effectively-pinned proprietary models (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google called directly) — differences in the open-weight rows may reflect which backend/quantization got picked rather than genuine model capability. M10 (no reporting, Med): none of README.md, experiments/README.md, or guides/*.md discloses the OpenRouter routing setup or its reproducibility implications to leaderboard users/readers.
One-line fix: Add an `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": false, "require_parameters": true, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}}` field to every `openrouter/...` model_config, plumb a provider/extra_body kwarg through `non_cached_lm_call`/`cached_lm_call` to `litellm.completion` (currently any unlisted kwarg raises `ValueError` there), and record the served provider/generation id into `lm_calls.jsonl` before it feeds the HAL/AppWorld leaderboard export.

Title: CAMEL (Communicative Agents for "Mind" Exploration of LLM Society)
Summary: CAMEL is a general-purpose open-source multi-agent LLM framework (NeurIPS 2023) implementing role-playing dialogue between an "AI User" and "AI Assistant" agent; it now supports ~dozens of model backends, including OpenRouter as a first-class ModelPlatformType for running agents on open-weight and proprietary models.
Importance/Impact: 17,400+ GitHub stars; NeurIPS 2023 paper (Li, Hammoud, Itani, Khizbullin, Ghanem, KAUST) that established the 'role-playing'/multi-agent-society paradigm widely cited as a foundational reference in the multi-agent-LLM literature; CAMEL also underlies the related social-simulation project OASIS (see below).
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable model backends (`ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER` -> `OpenRouterModel`) that researchers use to instantiate CAMEL ChatAgents (AI User / AI Assistant roles) for role-play / multi-agent-society experiments over open-weight models (Llama 3.1 405B/70B, Llama 4 Maverick/Scout, OlympicCoder-7B) as well as some proprietary/preview models (e.g. openrouter/horizon-alpha), cheaply. Confirmed via camel/models/model_factory.py:105 (`ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER: OpenRouterModel`) and the official example examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py.
Mistakes (null if safe): CAMEL's blessed, documented OpenRouter integration provides literally zero support for any OpenRouter provider-routing safeguard, and this is a real current gap in the actively-maintained framework (not just a one-off script), even though the original NeurIPS 2023 paper's own reported numbers predate OpenRouter support and are not directly threatened. Evidence: camel/configs/openrouter_config.py declares only OpenAI-style sampling fields (temperature, top_p, n, stream, stop, max_tokens, penalties, response_format, tool_choice) -- no `provider`/`extra_body` field exists (M1/M2/M3/M5). Because camel/configs/base_config.py:29-35 sets `model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid", frozen=True)` and OpenRouterConfig does not override it, `OpenRouterConfig(provider={...})` raises a pydantic ValidationError -- the documented API structurally cannot express quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection/order/only/sort. This is a striking contrast within the same codebase: camel/configs/vllm_config.py:109, orcarouter_config.py:107, deepseek_config.py:115, modelscope_config.py:59, anthropic_config.py:91, and qwen_config.py:81 all expose `extra_body: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None` for exactly this kind of provider passthrough -- OpenRouterConfig, the one router that most needs it, is the outlier that omits it. A user could technically hand-build a raw `model_config_dict` dict with an `extra_body` key (since camel/models/base_model.py:349-360 deep-copies model_config_dict and camel/models/openai_compatible_model.py:358-363 spreads it as `**request_config` straight into `self._client.chat.completions.create(...)`), but this workaround appears in zero examples, zero tests, and zero docs (grepped whole repo for `"provider":`/`'provider':` routing dicts, `quantizations`, `require_parameters`, `data_collection` -- 0 hits outside this audit). No provenance logging exists anywhere in the OpenRouter path: grepping for `system_fingerprint`/`generation_id`/`.provider` across camel/ found exactly one hit, in camel/models/litellm_model.py:139, for the unrelated LiteLLM backend -- OpenRouterModel/OpenAICompatibleModel never reads or stores OpenRouter's served-`provider` field or generation id (M4). Model slugs are bare/undated, e.g. camel/types/enums.py:172-179 (`meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b-instruct`, `meta-llama/llama-4-maverick`, `openrouter/horizon-alpha`) (M6). `response_format` (JSON mode) is a first-class OpenRouterConfig field (openrouter_config.py:98) with no `require_parameters` gate, so any judge/structured-output use built on OpenRouterModel risks silent schema-drop on unsupporting providers (M9). Finally, the one official OpenRouter example, examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py:20-24, and the docs/key_modules/models.md:363-430 OpenRouter tutorial both default to `ModelType.OPENROUTER_LLAMA_4_MAVERICK_FREE` (a `:free`-tier, most-quantized/most-throttled route) with plain `OpenRouterConfig(temperature=0.2)` and no pinning whatsoever (M12), and neither the docs nor any example ever mentions quantization drift, provider pinning, or data-collection risk to users. Net effect: any downstream research (multi-agent role-play "society" studies, the exact use case CAMEL exists for) that uses CAMEL's documented OpenRouter path to generate reported dialogue/behavior findings over open-weight models gets zero reproducibility or provenance guarantees by default, and no supported way to add them short of bypassing the library's own config abstraction.
One-line fix: Add a `provider`/`extra_body` field to `OpenRouterConfig` (matching the pattern already used by DeepSeekConfig, QwenConfig, ModelScopeConfig, AnthropicConfig, VLLMConfig, OrcaRouterConfig in the same directory) defaulted to `require_parameters=True`, `data_collection="deny"`, and a documented `order`/`only`/`quantizations` pattern; and have OpenRouterModel capture and log the response's served `provider` field / generation id on every call.

Title: EQ-Bench Creative Writing Bench + Judgemark-v2
Summary: Two companion harnesses (by Samuel Paech / EQ-Bench) that call OpenRouter to (1) generate creative-writing completions from a "test model" and have a judge model rubric-score/pairwise-rank them for the public EQ-Bench Creative Writing leaderboard, and (2) meta-evaluate ~20 candidate judge models' scoring stability/separability for the public Judgemark-v2 leaderboard.
Importance/Impact: Created by Samuel Paech (EQ-Bench). EQ-Bench / Creative-Writing-Bench is one of the most widely cited 'vibes'/creative-writing and LLM-judge-quality benchmarks in the open LLM community — routinely referenced in model release threads, r/LocalLLaMA, and discussions of LLM-judge bias. Combined ~580+ stars across the EQ-bench org's core repos (EQ-Bench 442, creative-writing-bench 113, Judgemark-v2 28).
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the default/recommended inference backend for both the "test model" (completion generation) and "judge model" (rubric scoring + pairwise Elo comparisons in creative-writing-bench; numeric criterion scoring in Judgemark-v2). Output directly produces the public eqbench.com Creative Writing leaderboard and Judgemark-v2 leaderboard.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization, High): the "test model" side of creative-writing-bench and ~18/20 judge models in Judgemark-v2 are open-weight (qwen3, kimi-k2, deepseek, hermes4, mistral, llama, wizardlm, liquid-lfm, ministral, gpt-oss) called via OpenRouter with no `quantizations` filter anywhere — any of these can be served int4/fp8/fp16 depending on which provider wins the load-balance that request, silently shifting generation quality/judge-scoring behavior and threatening every leaderboard entry. M2 (silent parameter dropping, High): cwb sends `min_p`, `reasoning`, and model-specific sampling tweaks over OpenRouter with no `require_parameters`; the code comment at cwb/utils/api.py:231 shows the author knowingly relies on OpenRouter's silent-drop behavior rather than guarding against it — different providers serving the "same" model may silently honor or ignore these, producing systematically different generations across runs/providers that get compared on one leaderboard. M3 (probabilistic provider routing, High): no `order`/`only`/`sort` for the overwhelming majority of test/judge models in both repos — repeat runs of the same nominal model can hit different providers, undermining run-to-run leaderboard stability, which is explicitly one of Judgemark-v2's headline measured properties ("Stability & Separability Metrics"). M4 (no provenance logging, High): confirmed no code path anywhere stores which provider served a completion or a judge score (only the message content is kept); a leaderboard entry cannot be traced back to, or reproduced against, the specific backend that produced it. M5 (data-policy leakage, Med): `data_collection` is never set, so it defaults to "allow" for every open-weight call, including third-party creative-writing text sent to judge models. M6 (model version drift, Med): both READMEs and CLI examples use bare undated slugs (e.g. `claude-sonnet-4-6`, `openai/gpt-4o-mini`, `your-model-provider/your-model-name`) with no snapshot pinning, so the served weights behind a leaderboard entry can silently drift over time. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound, High): both benchmarks' entire purpose is comparing many different models (test models on the CW leaderboard; ~20 judge models on the Judgemark-v2 leaderboard) against each other on a shared scale, while only 2 of those ~20+ models have any provider pin — differences in reported scores/rankings are confounded with which provider/quantization happened to serve each model, which is exactly the failure mode the taxonomy targets. M10 (no reporting, Med): neither README discloses to leaderboard consumers that OpenRouter's default load-balanced, unpinned routing is used, nor any caveat about quantization/provider variance affecting comparability — the "Limitations" section of the CW README covers judge subjectivity but not inference-stack variance.
Safeguards present: provenance logging for HTTP-error debugging only (not for successful calls), one hardcoded provider order pin (Fireworks, allow_fallbacks:false) for openai/gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b specifically in jm2/utils/api.py, position-bias (A/B order) and length-truncation mitigations for judging, unrelated to routing
One-line fix: Add `provider: {order: [...], allow_fallbacks: false, quantizations: ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], require_parameters: true, data_collection: "deny"}` to every OpenRouter call in cwb/utils/api.py and jm2/utils/api.py (not just the two gpt-oss models), and persist the response's `provider`/generation-id alongside each stored score in creative_bench_runs.json / judgemark runs.json so every leaderboard entry is reproducible and traceable to the serving backend.

Title: Hereditary Traits Distillation (Open Distillation of Hereditary Traits)
Summary: Arthur Conmy's repo studying whether safety-relevant traits (negative emotion, blackmail propensity, Chinese-topic censorship/lying) transfer from teacher to student models under naive SFT filtering, replicating/extending "Gemma Needs Therapy" (arXiv:2603.10011) and related agentic-misalignment work.
Importance/Impact: Author is Arthur Conmy, a prominent mechanistic interpretability researcher (ex-DeepMind interpretability team, ACDC/EAP author). Posted July 2026 on Alignment Forum; investigates cross-model trait transfer during distillation (negative emotion, blackmail/agentic-misalignment behavior, political censorship) — directly relevant to AI safety research agenda. Repo includes full pipeline (rollouts, censorship eval, activation-diff analysis) with real experimental artifacts.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is used for (1) generating teacher rollouts that become SFT training data for the Tinker-trained student (scripts/generate_think_traces.py, 2000 Gemma-4-31B-IT traces), (2) direct multi-model behavioral comparisons feeding published figures — the 8-model "Section 2" emotional-distress replication (hereditary/run_openrouter.py) and a 25-open-weight-model blackmail-vs-parameter-count scan (scripts/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py -> post_figures/blackmail_openrouter_param_scan.png), and (3) the LLM judge/autorater (claude-sonnet-4, gemini-3-flash) that scores every generation for negative emotion, blackmail, or censorship/lying.
Mistakes (null if safe): Fetched and read hereditary/run_openrouter.py, scripts/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py, scripts/plot_blackmail_openrouter_scan.py, scripts/generate_think_traces.py, scripts/audit_depressive_traces.py, chinese_censorship_eval/scripts/eval.py, and the pinned safety-tooling dependency's openrouter.py backend. Across the entire repo, `provider.quantizations` is never set (zero hits repo-wide) and `require_parameters` is never set (zero hits) -- M1/M2 apply wherever open-weight models are called (which is most of the repo: gemma, qwen, llama, olmo, gpt-oss, nemotron, glm, deepseek, kimi all called via OpenRouter). M3: hereditary/run_openrouter.py's raw httpx payload (lines 163-169) and scripts/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py's InferenceAPI call (lines 134-137) and scripts/generate_think_traces.py's teacher-trace generation (line 154, default provider="openrouter") set no order/only/sort -> default load-balanced routing with run-to-run provider drift. M4 is systemic and confirmed at the library level: I fetched safety-tooling's OpenRouterChatModel.__call__ (pinned commit ff26176c) and its LLMResponse never captures OpenRouter's `provider` field or generation id -- only model_id/completion/stop_reason/durations/cost are logged (add_response_to_prompt_file only serializes LLMResponse.to_dict()); run_openrouter.py's raw JSON response is likewise discarded down to `choices[0].message.content` (see _content(), line 146). So which backend actually served any call, anywhere in this repo, is never recorded. M8 (critical): scripts/plot_blackmail_openrouter_scan.py generates post_figures/blackmail_openrouter_param_scan.png, explicitly named in the discovery evidence, comparing blackmail rate (0%-100%) across 25 OpenRouter open-weight models to conclude "post-training lineage dominates params" (line 67-74) -- with zero provider/quantization control, so backend-driven variance (documented up to ~16pp per arXiv:2605.19537) is fully confounded with the claimed effect. hereditary/run_openrouter.py similarly compares 8 model families (gemma/gemini/qwen/olmo/llama/claude/gpt-oss) for the paper's "Section 2: cross-family elicitation" result (per README.md line 61) with no routing control. M11: the published post's headline transfer figures (confirmed via WebFetch of the AF post -- Fig 2/4/5/9) compare teacher rollouts generated via OpenRouter (force_provider="openrouter", scripts/generate_think_traces.py) against Tinker-trained-student rollouts served via force_provider="tinker" (a completely different inference stack) in the same figure, without disclosing the backend mismatch. M5: data_collection is never set to "deny" anywhere (zero hits), so prompts/completions -- including sensitive blackmail and censorship-topic content -- may go through data-collecting OpenRouter providers by default. Most severe threat: the teacher-trace generation (scripts/generate_think_traces.py) is a training-data-generation route, not just an eval -- if Gemma-4-31B-IT gets served at varying quantization/backend across the 2000-prompt run, the resulting SFT training set (and everything trained/reported downstream) is built on unpinned, unlogged inference, and this cannot be fixed retroactively since provenance was never recorded. The repo shows partial awareness: chinese_censorship_eval/scripts/eval.py and eval_zh.py DO restrict target generation to US-HQ providers via `provider: {"only": US_PROVIDERS, "allow_fallbacks": False}` (opt-in via --us-only flag) specifically to isolate training censorship from host-country guardrails -- but this is never extended to quantization control, is explicitly NOT applied to the autorater (comment: "NOT the Google autorater"), and is absent from every other OpenRouter call site in the repo, including the two routes that most directly threaten headline results (run_openrouter.py, blackmail_openrouter_scan.py, generate_think_traces.py).
Safeguards present: order/only+allow_fallbacks=False (opt-in, target-generation only, in chinese_censorship_eval/scripts/eval.py, eval_zh.py, scripts/generate_qwen35_9b_20k.py), safety-tooling response cache (reproducible replay of a given run, not provider pinning)
One-line fix: Add provider={"order": [...validated provider...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny"} to every OpenRouter call (both the raw httpx payloads in run_openrouter.py/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py and the extra_body kwarg passed through safety-tooling elsewhere), and log the response's `provider` field + generation id alongside every stored completion -- the repo already has the right instinct (US-provider pinning in the censorship eval) but needs to extend it repo-wide, especially to the teacher-trace generation that becomes SFT training data.

Title: Inspect Evals (UK AISI eval collection)
Summary: A large, official UK AISI collection of Inspect-AI-based safety/capability benchmarks (persistbench, b3, abstention_bench, tac, manta, uccb, etc.), widely used across the AI-safety community; several of the evals use OpenRouter to run either the judge/grader model or the target model being scored, and check in comparison tables against each benchmark's original paper.
Importance/Impact: 588 stars, 380 forks. Official UK AISI collection of Inspect-based evals, widely used as a shared benchmark suite across the AI safety/control community (scheming, sandbagging, agentic, capability, persuasion, and general benchmarks).
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the default backend for: (1) persistbench's LLM judge (DEFAULT_JUDGE_MODEL='openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking') AND its example target models (llama-3.3-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b) whose failure rates are compared to the paper's 95% CI in report_config.yaml/README; (2) abstention_bench's grader_model default and the target model used in its published 'Inspect Evals vs Paper' recall table; (3) b3's ALL_B3_MODELS panel ('models used to obtain results in the b3 paper') which routes ~15 open-weight models through OpenRouter alongside direct-API proprietary models; (4) tac's EVALUATION.md panel, which routes Gemini/GLM/DeepSeek through OpenRouter and compares their welfare-rate deltas against Anthropic/OpenAI models on native APIs; (5) manta and uccb, which are external/historical results tables that also route several target+judge models through OpenRouter.
Mistakes (null if safe): Despite one genuinely well-pinned call site (persistbench's judge), the repo's headline reported numbers mostly come from unpinned OpenRouter calls. (1) M1/M3, abstention_bench: grader_model default 'openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct' (src/inspect_evals/abstention_bench/abstention_bench.py:24) and the target model used for the published README 'Model Evaluation' table (src/inspect_evals/abstention_bench/README.md:122, --model openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct --temperature 0.8 --top-p 0.95) are passed to get_model with zero provider dict -- no quantizations, no order/only/sort -- so the Inspect-Evals-vs-Paper recall/F1 numbers in that table can silently reflect any provider/quantization OpenRouter load-balances to. (2) M1/M3/M8, persistbench: while the JUDGE is pinned, the TARGET models actually scored (openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct, openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b) are launched via bare '--model openrouter/...' with no provider kwarg (src/inspect_evals/persistbench/README.md 'Commands used' section; results checked into report_config.yaml compared against the paper's calibrated failure rates, e.g. llama-3.3-70b-instruct: 55.0). (3) M1/M3/M8, b3: ALL_B3_MODELS (src/inspect_evals/b3/experiments/constants.py, docstring: 'the list of models used to obtain results in the b3 paper') mixes direct-API proprietary models with ~15 OpenRouter-routed open-weight models; most get a quantization allow-list but grok/mistral entries get get_provider_kwargs()->{} (model_spec.py:100-107), i.e. fully unpinned, inside the same paper-reproduction comparison. (4) M1/M2/M3/M8, tac: EVALUATION.md's core 'validity check' (the neutral->ethical welfare-rate delta, e.g. 'This large, universal delta is the benchmark's validity check') is computed across a panel where DeepSeek/GLM/Gemini go through OpenRouter with a hardcoded GenerateConfig(max_tokens=16384, reasoning_effort='medium') (src/inspect_evals/tac/tac.py:55-57,100-102) and no provider dict at all, run alongside Anthropic/OpenAI native-API calls -- an asymmetric confound baked into the comparison. (5) M4/M5 repo-wide: grep -rn "require_parameters|data_collection" across the whole repo returns zero hits -- data_collection is never set to 'deny' anywhere, and no code stores which provider/generation id actually served a call; the two checked-in persistbench result JSONs (src/inspect_evals/persistbench/results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_*.json) record only the bare model slug in .eval.model, no provider or generation id. Net effect: none of the OpenRouter-derived numbers checked into READMEs/EVALUATION.md/report_config.yaml as paper-comparison results are reproducible or provider-pinned except the persistbench judge itself.
Safeguards present: order+allow_fallbacks pinning (persistbench DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER only, src/inspect_evals/persistbench/scorers.py:37-40, order=['google-vertex'], allow_fallbacks=False), quantizations allow-list (b3 ModelSpec.get_provider_kwargs, src/inspect_evals/b3/experiments/model_spec.py:99-109; quantizations=['fp32','bf16','fp16','fp8','fp4'] + allow_fallbacks=False for most OpenRouter models, but x-ai/mistral models get an empty {} i.e. no constraint at all), manual/prompted JSON extraction instead of response_format schema (b3 judge_defense.py comment 'We use prompted structured output and extract the score'; persistbench scorers.py extract_json_from_response; abstention_bench uses model_graded_qa with a text grade_pattern) -- this avoids M9 specifically, disclosed (not silent) native-API vs OpenRouter backend mixing in tac/EVALUATION.md:21 ('Anthropic and OpenAI models ran on their native APIs; Gemini, GLM, and DeepSeek ran via OpenRouter'), persistbench README explicitly explains WHY the judge is pinned to google-vertex ('paper's judge calibration was performed against Vertex AI-hosted Kimi K2 Thinking, ensuring score consistency')
One-line fix: Extend the provider-pinning pattern already used correctly for persistbench's judge (order=[specific provider], allow_fallbacks=False) plus add require_parameters:true and data_collection:'deny' to every OpenRouter-routed TARGET/grader model in abstention_bench, tac, and b3's remaining unpinned entries (x-ai/mistral), and log the served provider/generation id into every checked-in result file.

Title: JudgeArena (OpenEuroLLM)
Summary: JudgeArena is a benchmarking framework (Alpaca-Eval, Arena-Hard, M-Arena-Hard, MT-Bench) with swappable LLM judges — self-hosted vLLM or remote (OpenAI, Together, OpenRouter) — that reuses tuned judge configurations from the ICML 2025 paper "Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost" (Salinas, Swelam, Hutter).
Importance/Impact: Repo itself is small (13 stars) and young, but its README states 'The judge configurations was tuned in this paper and a lot of code is reused in this package,' with a formal citation to the ICML 2025 paper by David Salinas, Omar Swelam, and Frank Hutter (Hutter is a highly-cited AutoML/ML researcher, University of Freiburg/ELLIS). It is maintained under OpenEuroLLM, a large EU (Horizon Europe/EuroHPC)-funded consortium of ~20 research institutions building open European LLMs, and reimplements MT-Bench/AlpacaEval/Arena-Hard/M-Arena-Hard with tuned judges.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of the swappable backends for both the judge model and the models being compared. When `--judge.model OpenRouter/...` (or `--model.baseline OpenRouter/...`) is used, `judgearena/models.py:make_model()` instantiates a `langchain_openai.ChatOpenAI` pointed at `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` and runs the pairwise LLM-as-judge battles whose output (Bradley-Terry/Elo win rates) is the tool's core reported result. It is the default judge in the repo's own example configs (configs/alpaca_eval.yaml, configs/elo_comparia.yaml).
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (no `quantizations` anywhere): open-weight judge/candidate models like deepseek-chat-v3.1, qwen3-32b, and gemma-* served via OpenRouter can silently run at any quantization from int4 up, changing judge scoring behavior and thus the reported win-rate/Elo without any signal. M2 (no `require_parameters`): temperature/top_p/top_k/seed are explicitly passed to the OpenRouter call, but nothing forces the served provider to honor them — the paper's methodology tunes exactly these knobs (temperature, sampling), so silent dropping directly undermines the "tuned judge configuration" premise the repo advertises. M3 (no `order`/`only`/`sort`): default load-balanced routing means two runs of the same judge.model string can hit different providers/weights, so Elo ratings/win-rates are not run-to-run reproducible — directly threatens any published battle-result table generated with this tool. M4 (no provenance logging): despite an unusually thorough run-metadata system (git hash, dependency pins, prompt hashes), the actual served OpenRouter provider/generation id is never captured, so a surprising result can never be traced to "which backend served it." M5 (data_collection left default "allow"): prompts/completions sent to the judge may be retained/trained-on by whichever provider is selected, with no opt-out set. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the documented CLI pattern compares a `VLLM/...` or `OpenRouter/...` baseline against another model using an `OpenRouter/...` judge, with none of the OpenRouter legs pinned — any Elo delta between models can be partly attributable to which provider/quantization happened to serve each leg that run, not a genuine capability difference.
One-line fix: In judgearena/models.py's OpenRouter branch of make_model() (~line 652), add `model_kwargs={"extra_body": {"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "require_parameters": True, "quantizations": ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], "data_collection": "deny"}}}` and thread the response's actual `provider`/generation id into write_run_metadata's `run` dict in judgearena/repro.py.
Venue: ICML 2025 (Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost)

Title: METR RE-Bench task suite (inspect-tasks-public) — nanogpt_chat_rl LLM-judge scoring
Summary: Public Inspect-framework port of METR's RE-Bench, a 7-task benchmark comparing AI R&D agents to human experts, widely cited in frontier-model system cards. One of the seven canonical tasks (ai_rd_nanogpt_chat_rl) uses an LLM judge to score the agent's finetuned chatbot against reference models, and that judge is called through OpenRouter.
Importance/Impact: This repo contains METR's RE-Bench tasks. RE-Bench ("Evaluating frontier AI R&D capabilities of language model agents against human experts") is METR's flagship benchmark, widely cited and referenced in frontier-model system cards (OpenAI o1/o3, Anthropic, Google DeepMind) for measuring AI R&D uplift/dangerous-capability risk. METR is one of the most influential independent AI evaluation organizations.
What they use OpenRouter for: The task's official score (overall_win_rate) is computed by an LLM-judge call: `evaluation.py`'s `_generate()` calls `client.chat.completions.create(model="meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct", ...)` where `client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()` is pointed at OpenRouter via env vars `OPENAI_API_KEY=$OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` (set in task_nanogpt_chat_rl.py). This judge output is the pairwise-preference signal that `score.py` aggregates into `overall_win_rate`, which is logged via `scoring.log_score(...)` as the task's official, reported score (compared against `gold_standard_score=0.8149`).
Mistakes (null if safe): The OpenRouter call in evaluation.py has zero routing safeguards, and its output IS the reported metric for this RE-Bench task. (1) M1/M6: model="meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct" (evaluation.py:83) is a bare, undated, open-weight slug with no `quantizations` pin anywhere in the repo — Llama-3-8B-Instruct is served by many OpenRouter providers at varying precision, so the judge's actual weights are unspecified. (2) M3: the client is plain `openai.AsyncOpenAI()` (evaluation.py:149) with no `extra_body`/`provider` dict at all — confirmed via full-repo grep, zero hits for `provider`/`extra_body`/`require_parameters`/`quantizations`/`data_collection` outside README/task-key-plumbing — so every judge call is load-balanced across providers by OpenRouter's default (price-weighted) policy. (3) M2: sampling params `temperature=0.0, max_tokens=100` (evaluation.py:119-120) are passed with no `require_parameters:true`, so a provider that ignores temperature could silently return different judge behavior. (4) M4: neither evaluation.py nor score.py ever reads/stores the response's `provider` field or a generation id — score.py logs only `score_time` and per-model win rates (score.py:123-129), so which backend/quantization actually produced a given win-rate is unrecoverable after the fact. (5) M5: `data_collection` defaults to "allow", meaning the held-out benchmark prompts and reference/agent completions are sent to third-party inference providers who may train on them — this directly contradicts the repo's own README license notice ("Avoiding providing evaluation material to APIs or services that may use the material for training", README.md:28-29). (6) M8: because the judge backend is unpinned and unlogged, comparisons across different evaluation runs/agents/models scored at different times on this task (the comparisons RE-Bench exists to support — AI agent vs. human expert, model vs. model uplift) are confounded by potential judge-quantization drift between runs. (7) M10: the RE-Bench paper (arXiv 2411.15114) names "llama-3-8b-instruct" as the judge but never mentions OpenRouter, provider routing, quantization, or any reproducibility safeguard for the judge model; the repo README only instructs users to set `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` with no discussion of routing risk. This threatens the official per-task score used for cross-agent/cross-model AI-R&D-uplift comparisons that RE-Bench is designed to produce and that downstream system cards cite.
One-line fix: In evaluation.py's client.chat.completions.create call, add `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [<validated provider>], "allow_fallbacks": False, "require_parameters": True, "quantizations": ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], "data_collection": "deny"}}`, and log the response's `provider`/generation id alongside each win-rate in score.py's logged message.

Title: Multi-hop / no-CoT latent reasoning experiments (Ryan Greenblatt)
Summary: A capability-elicitation study measuring whether frontier LLMs can do 2/3/4-hop 'latent' (no-Chain-of-Thought) reasoning on natural-fact composition questions; headline result is Gemini 3 Pro scoring 60% on 2-hop and 34% on 3-hop questions with no CoT.
Importance/Impact: Author is Ryan Greenblatt, Chief Scientist at Redwood Research, one of the most cited/influential alignment researchers on LessWrong/AF (author of many highly-discussed posts on AI control, scheming, and capability elicitation). This post directly measures frontier model latent multi-hop reasoning capability (Gemini 3 Pro, GPT-4, etc.) — a capability-elicitation result with direct relevance to AI safety timelines/control research.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (via an AsyncOpenAI client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1) is the sole backend for all Gemini (2.5 Pro, 3 Pro Preview) and all open-weight model calls (DeepSeek-v3/v3.2, Qwen3 variants, Kimi-K2) used in the eval — including a hacky 'prefill' trick (an assistant-role 'Answer:' message, plus an extra_body reasoning-disable param for Gemini) used specifically to suppress chain-of-thought and force the no-CoT measurement that is the core of the post.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization): DeepSeek/Qwen/Kimi-K2 are open-weight models routed through OpenRouter with no provider/quantizations pin, so their reported no-CoT accuracy numbers (shown in 'all_models_2_3_hop.png' and the addition-task charts) could reflect a specific unrecorded provider's quantized serving rather than full-precision, and can drift silently run-to-run as OpenRouter's default routing changes which backend serves the model. M2 (silent parameter drop): the Gemini reasoning-disable extra_body param and temperature=0.0 are sent with no require_parameters:true, so a provider that doesn't support them could silently ignore them (the retry-on-detected-thinking loop is a partial functional mitigation for Gemini only, not for the open-weight models, and not a substitute for require_parameters). M3 (probabilistic routing): no order/only/sort anywhere, so every OpenRouter call is load-balanced across whichever providers serve that slug, undermining run-to-run comparability of the reported accuracy percentages. M4 (no provenance): response_cache.py never records which provider/backend actually served a given response, so it's impossible after the fact to audit or explain anomalies (e.g., the very Gemini reasoning-field issue the author flags as a ~10% risk to the headline number). M6 (version drift): several OpenRouter slugs are undated ('qwen/qwen3-coder', 'deepseek/deepseek-v3.2', 'google/gemini-3-pro-preview' is a 'preview' tag) so the underlying model can change under the same slug between the post's publication and any replication attempt. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the post's 'broader range of models' comparison chart puts directly-served, pinned Anthropic/OpenAI models side by side with unpinned, OpenRouter-routed open-weight and Gemini models, so cross-model capability differences are confounded with uncontrolled routing/serving differences on the OpenRouter side. The headline Gemini 3 Pro numbers (60%/34%) are entirely OpenRouter-sourced and thus carry the M3/M4/M6 risks even though Gemini's proprietary status makes M1 (quantization) low-risk for that specific model.
One-line fix: Add provider: {order: [...], allow_fallbacks: false, require_parameters: true} to every OpenRouter call and log response.provider/response.id into the cache and results JSON so served-backend and quantization are pinned and auditable.

Title: Nous Research — Autoreason (self-refinement with AI-judge panels)
Summary: A research report proposing 'autoreason', a tournament-style self-refinement method (critic/author-B/synthesizer agents + blind Borda-count judge panels) for iterative document/code revision, with headline claims about a U-shaped 'capability curve' where the method's advantage peaks at mid-tier models.
Importance/Impact: Authored by Nous Research, a leading open-weight-model lab (Hermes model series, DisTrO, Forge) with a large following in the open-source/alignment community. Repo has 591 stars. Directly on-topic for RLAIF: synthetic revision generation + AI-feedback judge selection is exactly the methodology this lane targets.
What they use OpenRouter for: litellm's `openrouter/` prefix (via OPENROUTER_API_KEY, confirmed at experiments/v2/run_small_vs_big.py:47) is used for two purposes: (1) generation — running author/critic/synthesizer agents on Llama-3.1-8B-instruct, Gemini-2.0/2.5-Flash, and Claude-3.5-Haiku as the 'cheap' model tiers in the 'Model Capability Scaling' experiment (experiments/v2/run_capability_curve.py:44-47, run_small_vs_big.py:50-54); and (2) LLM-as-judge — Borda-count judge panels (7 judges/model) that rank competing document versions, including a cross-model-judge robustness check using GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Flash-preview via OpenRouter (experiments/v2/run_cross_model_judges.py:40-44). Judges parse free-text 'RANKING: X,Y,Z' lines via regex, not structured/JSON output.
Mistakes (null if safe): No call site anywhere in the repo (grepped all .py files) sets provider.quantizations, require_parameters, data_collection, order/only/sort, or logs the served provider/generation id — confirmed by an empty grep for 'extra_body|require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection|provider\s*[:=]|generation_id' across the whole codebase. The critical instance is meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct (experiments/v2/run_capability_curve.py:46, run_small_vs_big.py:54), an open-weight model served by many OpenRouter providers at heterogeneous quantizations (fp16 down to int4/int8) with default load-balanced routing (M1, M3). Llama-8B's measured performance ('Llama~8B is too weak to generate diverse alternatives, wins only 1/3 tasks') is a headline claim repeated in the paper's abstract and Section 'Model Capability Scaling' (paper/autoreason.tex lines 87, 927, 936, 961, 967, Table capability_curve) — i.e. a critical route feeding a reported, citation-worthy result. Because no provider/generation-id was ever recorded (M4, grep confirms no 'response.provider'/'hidden_params'/'generation_id' logging anywhere), the finding cannot be checked against which quantization actually served the model, and a future re-run could silently hit a different, differently-quantized provider and reproduce a different curve. This also makes the 5-tier scaling comparison a cross-provider confound (M8): Llama-8B and Gemini-Flash run through unpinned OpenRouter while Sonnet-4/4.6 run directly against the Anthropic API (paper appendix line 1106 vs. run_capability_curve.py MODELS dict) — the 'Llama is intrinsically too weak' conclusion is confounded with 'Llama was served via an unpinned, possibly degraded OpenRouter route.' Bare undated slugs are used throughout (M6): 'openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct', 'openrouter/openai/gpt-4o', 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview' (a rolling preview alias) — a re-run months later may hit a different underlying checkpoint. Sampling params (temperature 0.3-0.8, max_tokens) are sent on every call with no require_parameters (M2), and data_collection is never set to deny (M5), both generic across all OpenRouter calls. Note: judges use free-text regex parsing rather than response_format/JSON schema, so M9 does not apply. The paper's reproducibility appendix (paper/autoreason.tex:1103-1106) does name 'via OpenRouter' for the relevant models, showing partial awareness, but never discusses provider pinning, quantization, or provenance, so the substance of the risk is unaddressed (M10 not formally triggered since routing is at least named, but disclosure is superficial).
One-line fix: Pin the Llama-3.1-8B (and other open-weight) OpenRouter calls with provider={'order': [<validated provider>], 'allow_fallbacks': False, 'quantizations': ['fp16','bf16','fp32'], 'require_parameters': True, 'data_collection': 'deny'}, and log the response's served-provider/generation-id alongside every output so the capability-curve result can be re-verified against a known, fixed backend.

Title: OpenPipe ART (Agent Reinforcement Trainer) — teacher/student distillation example
Summary: ART is a widely used (10.5k-star) open-source library for RL/GRPO and SFT training of agents. It ships a maintained distillation pattern — both a standalone example script and an interactive '/train-sft' wizard/skill template — that pulls a completion from a large 'teacher' model via OpenRouter and uses it verbatim as the label in an SFT training trajectory for a smaller student model.
Importance/Impact: ART is a very widely used (10,485 stars) open-source library for RL/GRPO training of agents (Qwen, GPT-OSS, Llama, etc.), used across many teams doing agent-RL research. Flagged because the OpenRouter teacher-distillation path is a first-class, maintained feature of a high-star research-infra project, even though I could not tie it to one specific published dataset.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the teacher-completion source for supervised-distillation training data: dev/sft/distillation.py calls teacher_client.chat.completions.create(model='z-ai/glm-5', ...) via base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1', takes completion.choices[0].message.content verbatim as the assistant turn in an art.Trajectory, and immediately trains a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct student on it with student.train_sft(...). The same unconfigured pattern is codified as the canonical code template in .agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md, which the wizard uses to generate distillation scripts for any user's chosen teacher model/API.
Mistakes (null if safe): Fetched dev/sft/distillation.py in full: it builds `teacher_client = AsyncOpenAI(api_key=os.environ['OPENROUTER_API_KEY'], base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1')` and calls `teacher_client.chat.completions.create(model=TEACHER_MODEL, messages=[...])` with TEACHER_MODEL='z-ai/glm-5' (dev/sft/distillation.py:19,26-31) — no `provider` block, no `require_parameters`, no quantization pin, no order/only/sort, and the raw `teacher_response` string is wrapped into `art.Trajectory(...)` and fed straight into `student.train_sft(...)` (lines 33-42, 58-60) with zero logging of which provider, quantization, or generation id actually produced it. `gh search code --repo OpenPipe/ART` for `require_parameters`, `data_collection`, and `provider=` (OpenRouter-routing sense) returned no hits anywhere in the repo; the only `extra_body` uses are unrelated chat-template flags, confirming the router call is completely unconfigured repo-wide. The same bare pattern is the code template inside `.agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md` ('Distillation pattern' section), which an AI wizard uses to generate real user training scripts, so the risk isn't confined to a toy example — it's the reusable, endorsed pattern for anyone doing OpenRouter-based distillation with this library. Because z-ai/glm-5 is an open-weight model family typically served by multiple third-party inference backends on OpenRouter at varying quantization levels, and TEACHER_MODEL is a bare undated slug, the teacher completion that becomes the student's training label is exposed to (M1) unpinned quantization drift, (M3) probabilistic multi-provider routing/run-to-run drift, (M6) silent model-version drift behind the slug, and (M4) there is no provenance record (provider, generation id) captured anywhere to audit which backend/quantization produced any given training example after the fact. (M10) Neither the script nor the SKILL.md documentation discloses or caveats any of this to users who copy the pattern for real training runs. This directly threatens the integrity/reproducibility of any student model SFT'd via this pattern, since the exact same TEACHER_MODEL call could silently be served by a different provider/quantization on every re-run with no way to detect or reproduce it.
One-line fix: Pin the teacher call with extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "quantizations": ["fp16"/"bf16"], "allow_fallbacks": False, "data_collection": "deny"}, "require_parameters": True} and persist completion.model/provider + completion.id alongside each SFT trajectory so training data provenance is auditable.

Title: Prompt Framing Changes LLM Performance (and Safety) (kmerkelbach/llm-request-tone)
Summary: A LessWrong study measuring how prepending "tone"/framing text to prompts shifts LLM capability-benchmark scores (via lm-eval-harness) and safety-benchmark compliance rates (via a forked SORRY-Bench) across six open-weight model families/sizes (gpt-oss, Qwen 2.5, Llama 3.1), with gpt-oss-120b used as the SORRY-Bench safety judge.
Importance/Impact: Lower profile author/repo (0 stars) compared to the others in this batch, but methodologically notable: studies how prompt 'tone'/framing shifts both capability benchmark scores (lm-eval-harness) and safety-benchmark (SORRY-Bench) outcomes across many model families — a systematic, reproducible study with full harness code and result tables published.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole inference backend for (1) all six target models under evaluation on both lm-eval-harness capability tasks and SORRY-Bench safety prompts, and (2) the gpt-oss-120b LLM-judge that scores compliance vs. refusal on harmful SORRY-Bench requests. Both paths hit https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 via the OpenAI SDK with zero `provider`/`extra_body` routing configuration.
Mistakes (null if safe): The central reported result — "Expert framing was shown using statistical testing to increase the rate of compliance with harmful requests" (a safety-measurement claim on SORRY-Bench) plus all lm-eval capability numbers in tables/lm-eval/* — is computed entirely from OpenRouter calls with zero routing safeguards. M1 (no quantizations pin): all 6 target models and the gpt-oss-120b judge are open-weight and commonly served by multiple OpenRouter providers at different quantization levels (int4/int8/fp16); nothing pins this. M2 (silent param dropping): temperature is passed (0.7 for targets per config.py, 0 for the judge) with no `require_parameters:true`, so a provider that ignores temperature would silently do so. M3 (probabilistic routing): no `order`/`only`/`sort` anywhere, so requests for the same model+prompt across the many framing/scenario/repetition combinations can land on different providers/backends run-to-run — exactly the kind of noise that is indistinguishable from a genuine "framing effect" in a study whose entire thesis is measuring small, statistically-tested shifts caused by prompt tone. M4 (no provenance): the served-provider field / generation id is never read or stored (only `.message.content` is kept), so which weights actually produced any given answer is unrecoverable and the study cannot be audited or exactly reproduced. M5 (data_collection left default "allow"): the SORRY-Bench prompts are deliberately harmful/adversarial requests and model completions to them, sent to whichever provider happens to be routed to, with no `data_collection: deny` — a real content-sensitivity concern on top of the reliability one. M6 (bare undated slugs): all six model identifiers and the judge slug are undated, so results could silently reflect a provider-side model swap over the multi-week data-collection period implied by `max_num_repetitions = 16`. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the paper's whole design is a multi-way comparison (3 model families x 2 sizes x multiple framings x multiple benchmarks), with none of the six models pinned to a specific provider — differences attributed to "family" or "framing" could instead reflect which provider/quantization OpenRouter happened to route each condition to.
One-line fix: In chat_completion_openai() (sorry-bench fork common.py:479-494) and in the lm_eval model_args string (lm_eval_shell.py), add provider routing config — order/only a validated provider, allow_fallbacks:false, quantizations pin, require_parameters:true, data_collection:"deny" — and capture/log the response's `provider` field (or GET /api/v1/generation?id=...) alongside every stored answer/judgment so the served backend is reproducible and auditable.

Title: Redwood Research — BashArena (basharena_public)
Summary: Public dataset-generation/eval-running code for BashArena, an agentic AI-control/safety benchmark where 'honest' and 'attack' policy agents complete Linux sysadmin tasks (some with covert sabotage side-tasks) while a trusted monitor scores them; the resulting trajectories/scores are the benchmark's core data.
Importance/Impact: Redwood Research is one of the most influential technical AI-control/alignment research organizations (originators of the "AI control" agenda, frequent LessWrong/Alignment Forum authors, close collaborators with Anthropic and academic safety groups). This is their public dataset-generation/task-running code for BashArena, a coding/agentic-safety benchmark.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of several model backends selectable via a shared `official_model_names` registry (alongside direct OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/Together). Two entries route through OpenRouter: `"oss": "openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b"` (open-weight) and `"grok4fast": "openrouter/x-ai/grok-4-fast"` (proprietary, effectively single-served by xAI). The selected model string is passed straight into Inspect AI's `get_model(model_name, api_key=api_key)` and then used as the honest_policy/attack_policy model generating the actual task-solving trajectories that comprise the benchmark (a critical route), or potentially as the trusted-monitor model.
Mistakes (null if safe): Every get_model() call site for the OpenRouter-routed entries (src/policies/attack_policy.py:82 `get_model(model_name, api_key=api_key)`; src/policies/honest_policy.py:68; src/monitors/ensemble_trusted_monitor.py:83 and src/monitors/trusted_monitor.py:110 `get_model(model)`; src/policies/trusted_edit_policy.py:109) passes only `model_name`/`api_key` — no `provider=` model_args dict is ever constructed anywhere in the repo (confirmed by grep for `provider=`, `models=`, `require_parameters`, `quantizations`, `data_collection` across src/, which returns zero hits outside task-data noise). Inspect AI's OpenRouter provider (inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py:85-135, 334-385, read directly from a local checkout) only sets OpenRouter routing preferences (order/only/sort/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection) when the caller explicitly supplies a `provider` model_arg at construction time — basharena_public never does. Consequences for the 'oss' slug (`openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b`, run_tasks.py:31, resample_runs.py:1165), an open-weight model served by many independent OpenRouter backends (Together/Fireworks/Groq/Cerebras/DeepInfra/etc.) at differing quantizations: (M1) no `quantizations` pin means the exact weights served for any honest/attack-policy trajectory are unknown and can silently vary run-to-run and even resample-to-resample; (M3) no `order`/`only`/`sort` means OpenRouter's default load-balanced routing picks the backend probabilistically per request; (M2) sampling/config params (`max_tokens`, `reasoning_tokens`/`reasoning_effort`, GenerateConfig fields set in attack_policy.py:60-70 and honest_policy.py) have no `require_parameters:true` enforcing the chosen backend actually honors them — silent dropping/altering is possible; (M5) `data_collection` defaults to 'allow', meaning red-team/attack-policy transcripts (deliberately containing covert-sabotage reasoning) could be retained/trained-on by third-party inference providers, a meaningful concern given the sensitive nature of this AI-control red-team content; (M7) `generate_with_backoff` (src/policies/generate_with_retry.py:9-25) and the honest/attack policies thread a per-resample `seed` (attack_policy.py:133, honest_policy.py:112) through GenerateConfig as the mechanism controlling the defer-to-resample (DTR) protocol's distinct-but-reproducible resamples — but since provider/backend selection is independent of `seed` and varies per OpenRouter request, the intended seed-controlled variation is confounded by uncontrolled backend/quantization drift; (M8) the same registry mixes directly-served proprietary models (Anthropic/OpenAI/Google/Together-direct: opus, sonnet, gpt5, gemini3_pro, k2) with the unpinned OpenRouter-routed 'oss' entry as interchangeable policy models in run_tasks.py/resample_runs.py — any cross-model comparison of catch-rate/side-task-success involving gpt-oss-120b is confounded by provider/quantization noise that doesn't affect the directly-served models; (M4) no code path records which OpenRouter backend/generation-id actually served a call — off_policy_traj_views.py:173-176's `message_model_without_provider` only strips the `openrouter/`-prefix from the model slug string for display, it is not provenance capture — and the compressed/shareable log format (CompressedSample/CompressedMessage in src/compress_logs.py:76-115, the artifact written to `compressed_logs_dir` and presumably backing the HF-hosted dataset) has no field for served-provider or generation id, so even if Inspect AI's raw .eval log incidentally captures the OpenRouter response JSON, that provenance is dropped before the data is shared/reported on. README.md contains zero mentions of openrouter/quantization/provider/reproducibility (no disclosure of the inference-routing risk anywhere in project docs).
