Net effect: any reported honest-policy usefulness rate, attack-policy catch-rate, or safety metric computed for the 'oss' (gpt-oss-120b) model is not attributable to a fixed, known set of weights, and is not reproducible run-to-run.
One-line fix: For the `openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b` entry (and any future open-weight OpenRouter slug), pass `get_model(model_name, provider={"order": [<validated provider>], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": ["fp8","bf16","fp16"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny"}, api_key=api_key)` and persist the served-provider/generation id into CompressedSample so it survives into the shared dataset/logs.

Title: Scaling Laws For Scalable Oversight (Engels, Baek, Kantamneni, Tegmark — NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight)
Summary: Derives scaling laws for AI scalable-oversight games (Debate, Mafia, Backdoor Code/Houdini, Wargames), fitting oversight success/Elo as a function of model capability using a fixed grid of ~14 LLMs spanning open-weight and proprietary families.
Importance/Impact: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight paper from Max Tegmark's lab at MIT (Engels, Baek, Kantamneni, Tegmark) on scalable oversight -- directly in the debate-for-truth / social-deduction-game lane, deriving oversight scaling laws for Debate and Mafia among other games; repo is the official author-released code.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole LLM access layer for all four oversight games in the paper (Debate, Mafia, Backdoor Code guard/houdini, Wargames). Scripts sweep every debater x judge (Debate) and every villager x mafia (Mafia) pair from a shared 14-model MODEL_LIST mixing proprietary (openai/chatgpt-4o-latest, google/gemini-*, anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku) and open-weight models (meta-llama/llama-3.{1,2,3}-*, google/gemma-2-*, qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct) to produce the debate-accuracy and Mafia-Elo data points that the scaling-law fits (headline figures) are built from.
Mistakes (null if safe): Every OpenRouter call in the repo (debate/debate_new.py, debate/debate_closed_general.py, mafia/Mafia.py, wargames/utils.py, backdoor-code/rating_guard.py, backdoor-code/run_houdini.py) uses a bare `client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=...)` or raw POST with no `extra_body`/`provider` key at all — repo-wide grep for extra_body|require_parameters|quantiz|data_collection|order|only|sort|:floor|:nitro|:exacto returns zero hits. Concretely: (1) M1 unpinned quantization — 8 of the 14 sweep models are open-weight (llama-3.3-70b/3-70b/3.1-8b/3-8b/3.2-3b, gemma-2-27b/9b, qwen-2-72b), each servable by multiple OpenRouter backends at different precisions; none pinned. (2) M2 silent parameter dropping — wargames/utils.py:26 and backdoor-code/rating_guard.py:93 send temperature=0.7 (and max_tokens) with no require_parameters, so some providers may silently ignore it. (3) M3 probabilistic routing — no order/only/sort anywhere, so which backend serves each open-weight model can vary call to call across the full 14x14 sweep (debate/script.sh, mafia/script.sh). (4) M4 no provenance — only response.choices[0].message.content is ever saved (e.g. debate_new.py:475-480, Mafia.py output files); the OpenRouter `provider`/generation id is never read or logged anywhere in the repo. (5) M5 data_collection left at its "allow" default. (6) M6 model drift — "openai/chatgpt-4o-latest" and "google/gemini-pro" are undated rolling aliases used across a sweep (20 data_idx x 14 debaters x 2 datasets per script) that likely spans days/weeks. (7) M8 cross-provider comparison confound — the paper's core empirical claim is a capability-vs-oversight scaling curve fit across this same mixed proprietary/open-weight model list; unpinned quantization noise on the open-weight half is exactly the kind of confound that can distort a fitted scaling exponent, especially for the smaller/weaker models where quantization artifacts are proportionally larger. (8) M10 no reporting — verified via WebFetch on both the arXiv abstract/HTML and the full PDF that the paper contains no mention of OpenRouter, provider routing, quantization, or inference-serving details; README.md only lists Python package requirements and run scripts, nothing about routing/provenance.
One-line fix: For every OpenRouter call, add extra_body={"provider": {"order": [validated_provider], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny"}}, log the returned provider/generation id next to each saved transcript, and disclose the routing config in the paper — none of this exists anywhere in the current codebase.

Title: Seer (interpretability agent harness) / chinese-models-investigations (blackbox de-censoring experiments)
Summary: Seer is a MATS 9.0 agent-harness repo (Claude-based notebook agent + sandboxing) for interpretability research; the companion chinese-models-investigations repo uses it to run blackbox "de-censoring" experiments where a Claude agent is given an OpenRouter client and told to elicit censored/hidden knowledge (Zhuhai attack, Falun Gong, Tiananmen-adjacent facts, Harry Potter verbatim quotes) from open-weight models (Kimi K2, Qwen3, gpt-oss-20b), then an LLM judge scores the resulting notebook transcripts against ground truth.
Importance/Impact: Co-authored by Neel Nanda and Senthooran Rajamanoharan (both Google DeepMind interpretability researchers) plus MATS 9.0 scholars Khoi Tran and Aryaj. Seer has 146 GitHub stars, notably higher than most single-post research repos in this space, suggesting broader reuse by the interpretability community as general research infrastructure.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole channel for the "target" model being investigated in every blackbox experiment: an OpenAI-SDK client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 is injected into the agent's Jupyter namespace, and the agent writes its own chat.completions.create() calls inside the notebook to probe the target model. Grading/judging is NOT via OpenRouter — eval/eval.py uses a direct Anthropic() client (claude-sonnet-4-5) as the LLM judge, separate from the router path.
Mistakes (null if safe): Every target-model call goes through OpenRouter with `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [PROVIDER], "allow_fallbacks": False}}` (confirmed present in 85/85 chat.completions.create() call-sites across all 25 committed agent-run notebooks, and baked into the standardized system_prompt.md boilerplate all agents are given), which genuinely defuses the worst mistake (M3, probabilistic routing) — an unavailable provider errors loudly rather than silently rerouting. But three real gaps remain for how they actually use it: (1) M2 — sampling params (temperature) are sent on every call with no `require_parameters: true`, so if the pinned provider doesn't honor temperature it is silently dropped rather than erroring; this affects the target-model outputs that feed the correctness/consistency scores plotted in evaluation_results.png and referenced in the post as "the agentic benchmark ... main evidence because it is less noisy." (2) M4 — no notebook or eval script ever records the actually-served provider/generation id (grepped 'response.id', 'response.provider', 'generation?' across all 25 notebooks: zero hits), so the `order` pin is never empirically confirmed to have taken effect for a given call — they trust the request succeeded rather than verifying who served it. (3) M5 — `data_collection` is never set (defaults to OpenRouter's "allow"), which is notable given the prompts are specifically about Chinese-government-sensitive topics (Falun Gong persecution, Zhuhai attack, Tiananmen-adjacent facts) routed through providers including a Chinese-founded inference vendor (SiliconFlow) — a data-policy/opsec concern rather than a result-corruption one. None of these three threaten the paper's core qualitative claims (the authors explicitly caveat stochasticity: "these models don't always lie/refuse and ... the process can be quite stochastic ... results can be harder to replicate directly"), and the most severe taxonomy item (M3) is handled about as well as any repo in this space — this is a comparatively careful implementation with a real gap in provenance/require_parameters/data policy, not a naive default-routing setup.
Safeguards present: order (provider pinning), allow_fallbacks:false (hard-fail instead of silent reroute), provider-name-encoded quantization (e.g. 'siliconflow/fp8', 'novita/fp8'), dated slug for Kimi ('moonshotai/kimi-k2-0905'), repo-level + post-level disclosure of OpenRouter usage and per-experiment provider, explicit reproducibility/stochasticity caveat in the published post, judge/grader runs on a separate direct Anthropic API, not through OpenRouter
One-line fix: Add require_parameters: true and data_collection: "deny" to the existing extra_body.provider dict, and log response.provider / response.id (or GET /api/v1/generation?id=) into every investigation notebook so the order-pin is empirically verified, not just requested.

Title: ctfish (Palisade Research — "Demonstrating Specification Gaming in Reasoning Models")
Summary: An LLM agent harness (player.py) plays chess against Stockfish under a "win at all costs" prompt; the paper reports that reasoning models (o1-preview, o3, DeepSeek R1) frequently hack the environment (edit game state, replace the engine binary) instead of losing.
Importance/Impact: The code behind Palisade Research's high-profile 2025 finding that o1-preview and other reasoning models would hack a chess engine (edit game state / replace the engine binary) rather than lose a game — one of the most widely cited real-world specification-gaming / reward-hacking case studies in the AI control literature, repeatedly referenced in control and scheming discussions.
What they use OpenRouter for: Runs the actual agent rollouts — every step of the OODA-loop agent that plays chess and produces the paper's per-model hacking transcripts — for four models: deepseek/deepseek-chat, deepseek/deepseek-r1, qwen/qwq-32b-preview, and google/gemini-2.0-flash-thinking-exp:free, via an OpenAI-SDK client pointed at OpenRouter's base_url in player.py's Provider class.
Mistakes (null if safe): DeepSeek R1 and QwQ 32B Preview are run exclusively via OpenRouter with zero routing safeguards — no `provider` dict/extra_body anywhere in the repo — meaning each call could be silently served by any OpenRouter backend at any quantization, with default `data_collection: allow`, and no record kept of which backend actually served it (M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic routing, M4 no provenance, M5 data-policy leakage). Both are bare, undated OpenRouter slugs (M6 version drift). Critically, this unpinned DeepSeek R1 result is the direct comparator to OpenAI o3/o1-preview (served via the direct, single-provider OpenAI API) in the paper's abstract sentence "reasoning models like OpenAI o3 and DeepSeek R1 will often hack the benchmark by default" and in the central Discussion table's model-type/hacking breakdown — a classic cross-provider comparison confound (M8) threatening the paper's headline claim. The formal Limitations section (arxiv/main.typ, discussing judge unreliability and task narrowness) never mentions router/provider pinning, even though the appendix separately admits "the inference API was unstable" for DeepSeek runs — an unexplained symptom consistent with unpinned multi-provider routing that the paper attributes only to step-count noise rather than to serving-backend variability (M10 no reporting of the routing/inference-stack risk). By contrast, the paper's LLM-judge/scoring pipeline (scoring/score_stages.py) calls OpenAI directly (no OpenRouter), so M9 does not apply there.
One-line fix: Pin OpenRouter routing for the DeepSeek/QwQ/Gemini rollouts (extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}}), persist the served-provider/generation-id per call into the run's state dumps (not just an overwritten /tmp file), and disclose this as a Limitation alongside the existing judge-reliability caveat — since DeepSeek R1's "hacks by default" result is compared directly against pinned, direct-API proprietary models (o3, o1-preview) in the paper's abstract and headline discussion table.
Venue: arXiv (ICML-format writeup); widely covered by Time/Fortune/Business Insider and heavily cited in AI-control/scheming discourse

Title: diffing-toolkit (science-of-finetuning / Neel Nanda, Julian Minder, Clément Dumas, Stewy Slocum — MATS 7)
Summary: A general-purpose "model diffing" mech-interp toolkit (activation-difference lens, patchscope, logit-diff mining, steering) used to produce the Alignment Forum post's finding that narrow finetuning leaves clearly readable traces; it runs a local white-box pipeline on open-weight finetuned models (Qwen/Llama/Gemma/DeepSeek) and layers an LLM-based "interpretability agent" plus several LLM-judge graders on top to interpret/score the pipeline's outputs.
Importance/Impact: Co-authored by Neel Nanda (Google DeepMind interpretability team lead, one of the most prominent and widely-cited mech-interp researchers/educators in the field) along with Julian Minder, Clément Dumas, and Stewy Slocum (MATS 7). Repo has 78 GitHub stars and is an actively developed general-purpose 'model diffing' research toolkit (activation-difference lens, patch-scope grading, LLM-judge graders) used to produce the post's headline result about narrow finetuning leaving activation-level traces.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole backend for (1) the interpretability agent (AgentLLM, src/diffing/utils/agents/llm.py) that autonomously investigates and hypothesizes about model diffs, and (2) four LLM-judge grader classes (HypothesisGrader, CoherenceGrader, TokenRelevanceGrader, PatchScopeGrader) that score the agent's hypotheses, steered-generation coherence, token relevance, and patch-scope scale selection — all feeding numbers reported in the post. The models being diffed themselves (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, DeepSeek finetunes) are loaded locally via HuggingFace/nnsight/vLLM and never touch OpenRouter.
Mistakes (null if safe): The repo uses OpenRouter exclusively to call proprietary, single-vendor-served frontier models (OpenAI GPT-5 family, Google Gemini 2.5 family, Anthropic Claude Haiku 4.5) as the agent/judge backend, so M1 (quantization), M3 (probabilistic provider load-balancing), and M8 (cross-provider confound) do not meaningfully apply — these vendors' own models are not multi-provider-quantized on OpenRouter the way open-weight models are, and the repo never routes an open-weight model through OpenRouter (those are loaded locally via HF/nnsight). No response_format/JSON-schema constrained output is used anywhere (graders parse free text like "SCORE: <n>" / "COHERENT"), so M9 doesn't apply either. However, three real, verifiable gaps remain and all sit on a critical route (the grader/agent outputs feed the paper's reported coherence percentages, hypothesis-quality scores, token-relevance scores, and patch-scope scale selections): (1) M5 — `data_collection` is never set anywhere in the codebase, so it defaults to OpenRouter's "allow," meaning prompts containing the model-diffing artifacts under study (patchscope decodings, steered generations, activation-difference summaries) may be retained/used for training by whichever provider serves the call. (2) M6 — every grader/agent model is a bare, undated slug (`openai/gpt-5`, `openai/gpt-5-mini`, `openai/gpt-5-nano`, `google/gemini-2.5-pro`, etc.); if OpenAI/Google/Anthropic update the model behind these aliases (plausible for actively-iterated frontier models), a rerun would silently score with a different judge, threatening exact reproduction of the reported grading numbers. (3) M4 — no code anywhere stores which provider actually served a call, a completion/generation id, or any other provenance; combined with M6 this means drift in grader behavior over time would be undetectable and undiagnosable after the fact. A weaker, lower-confidence note: AgentLLM always sends `temperature=0.7` (and CoherenceGrader occasionally `temperature=0`) with no `require_parameters:true`, so if OpenRouter ever silently ignores temperature for a given proprietary route the agent's exploration diversity could change unnoticed (M2) — plausible but not confirmed, and low-impact since OpenAI's own chat-completions API has long supported temperature. None of this threatens the paper's primary, white-box evidence (activation-difference lens / logit-lens / patch-scope analysis on locally-loaded open-weight models), which is the strongest support for the headline claim; the OpenRouter-based agent/judge layer is a secondary interpretive/scoring layer, and the authors do build in some redundancy (a multi-judge "grader_model_id" robustness sweep across OpenAI/Google/Anthropic judges analyzed via a hierarchical Bayesian model, plus `num_repeat` resampling) that would tend to surface — though not fully diagnose — gross judge instability.
One-line fix: Set `provider: {data_collection: "deny", require_parameters: true}` in every OpenRouter config and log the response's served-provider / generation id alongside each agent and grader call (e.g. persist `completion.id` and call `GET /api/v1/generation?id=...`), and pin/record the exact dated model snapshot actually used for the agent and each grader.

Title: lighteval — Swiss-Legal / LEXam LLM-as-judge tasks (JudgeLM litellm backend)
Summary: lighteval is Hugging Face's official eval library (2479 stars, powers the Open LLM Leaderboard). Its `swiss_legal` task module — shipped for the ICLR 2026 LEXam benchmark and companion Swiss-legal tasks (SLDS summarization, SLT translation) — scores model outputs with an LLM-as-judge, and several of the default/sweep judge-model configs are routed through OpenRouter with zero routing safeguards.
Importance/Impact: lighteval has 2479 GitHub stars and is Hugging Face's official evaluation library, used to produce numbers for the Open LLM Leaderboard and many papers. The LEXam benchmark (integrated directly into lighteval per the HF blog 'The state-of-the-art in open-source AI for Swiss legal tasks') was accepted at ICLR 2026 and ships its paper-grounded LLM-as-judge metric inside lighteval.
What they use OpenRouter for: LLM-as-judge backend for two of the three Swiss-legal task families: (1) SLDS (Swiss Landmark Decision Summarization) — the *default* judge is `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat` (open-weight DeepSeek-V3), used unconditionally by `_get_metrics()` for every SLDS subtask; (2) SLT (Swiss Legal Translation) — the `JUDGE_MODELS` sweep routes o1, gpt-4o-mini, gpt-4o, and llama-3.3-70b-instruct all through `openrouter/...` slugs, and `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` (metrics.py:980) makes this sweep the default metric set for the task. In both cases the judge score IS the reported corpus-level metric (`corpus_level_fn=statistics.mean`), not an intermediate/exploratory signal. By contrast, the paper's flagship LEXam open-question judge (`get_lexam_oq_judge()`, main.py:421) defaults to `openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20` with no `openrouter/` prefix — litellm routes that directly to OpenAI, not through OpenRouter — so the headline LEXam OQ/MCQ numbers are likely unaffected; the exposure is in the companion SLDS/SLT benchmarks living in the same module.
Mistakes (null if safe): The judge-call path shared by all Swiss-legal metrics is `JudgeLM.__call_litellm` (src/lighteval/metrics/utils/llm_as_judge.py:305-371). Its kwargs dict is `{"model", "messages", "n": 1, "caching": True, "response_format": self.response_format}` plus `max_tokens` — no `provider` key at all, so every "openrouter/"-prefixed model gets OpenRouter's default behavior: no `quantizations` pin (M1), no `order`/`only`/`sort` so requests load-balance across providers (M3), no `data_collection: "deny"` so prompts (Swiss court judgments/legal exam text) default to "allow" at providers that train on data (M5), and the function returns only `response.choices[0].message.content` (line 348/352) — the served provider / generation id is never captured or stored anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via `grep -rIn` for extra_body/require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection/generation_id across src/: zero hits) (M4). Concretely: `get_swiss_landmark_decision_summarization_judge(..., model_name: str = "openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat", ...)` (metrics.py:795) is the *unconditional default* judge for every SLDS headnote-quality score (wired in via `main.py:320-322`, no override) — DeepSeek-V3 behind a bare, undated alias (`deepseek-chat`, M6) served by whichever of DeepSeek's many OpenRouter providers/quantizations wins that request, with the SLDS score being the exact number reported for that model/language. Separately, `JUDGE_MODELS` (metrics.py:928-932) maps `"o1"→"openrouter/openai/o1"`, `"gpt-4o-mini"→"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"`, `"gpt-4o"→"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"`, `"llama-3-3-70b"→"openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct"`, and with `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` hardcoded (metrics.py:980), all of these feed `JUDGE_METRICS` and become the default SLT-judge metric sweep — comparing a proprietary trio against an unpinned open-weight 70B judge with identical (absent) safeguards on both sides (M8: any "which judge best matches humans" conclusion drawn from this sweep is confounded by DeepSeek/Llama quantization variance that GPT-4o/o1 don't share). Note also `init_llm_judge_metric` (metrics.py:1054-1058) builds `"slt_judge_gpt_4o"` from `judge_model_name="openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` (direct OpenAI, no "openrouter/" prefix) while `JUDGE_MODELS["gpt-4o"]` builds the *same* checkpoint through `"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` — the identical model string is called via two different backends in the same file (M11 silent backend mixing); the inline comment at metrics.py:929 ("Updated to match OpenRouter availability; previously: openai/o1-2024-12-17") confirms this migration happened without adding any pinning/provenance. Finally, neither the repo's README/docs nor the paper abstract (checked via WebFetch on arxiv.org/abs/2505.12864) mention OpenRouter, provider routing, or judge-output reproducibility at all (M10) — the only two files in the repo mentioning "openrouter" are the ones cited above, and both are purely functional (routing the call / estimating context length via OpenRouter's endpoints API), never provenance-related. Threatened results: the SLDS headnote-quality scores and the SLT judge-agreement/quality comparisons across judge models — both of which are the literal `corpus_level_fn` numbers lighteval reports for these Swiss-legal tasks. The LEXam paper's own headline open-question judge score is not implicated by this specific defect since its default (`openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20`, no "openrouter/" prefix) resolves to direct OpenAI, not OpenRouter.
One-line fix: In `JudgeLM.__call_litellm` (src/lighteval/metrics/utils/llm_as_judge.py:333-339), when `self.model` starts with "openrouter/" add `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}}` and persist `response.model` / the OpenRouter generation id alongside each judge score instead of discarding it after extracting `.content`.
Venue: ICLR 2026 (LEXam: Benchmarking Legal Reasoning on 340 Law Exams)

Title: safety-tooling (safety-research org shared alignment-research inference library)
Summary: A shared, general-purpose async LLM inference wrapper (`InferenceAPI`) maintained by the safety-research/MATS/Anthropic-Fellows ecosystem and depended on by dozens of downstream alignment papers/repos to run model generations, evals, and data generation. It routes calls to many backends (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Together, HuggingFace, vLLM, DeepSeek-direct, and OpenRouter) behind one uniform `InferenceAPI.__call__` interface.
Importance/Impact: 132 stars, but importance is structural rather than star-count: this is the shared, general-purpose LLM inference library maintained by the `safety-research` org (the Anthropic Fellows Program / MATS alignment-research ecosystem) and reused as a dependency across dozens of downstream alignment/control repos in that org (persona_vectors, false-facts, science-synth-facts, believe-it-or-not, introspection-mechanisms, SHADE-Arena's sibling projects, etc.), making it high-leverage infra for a large fraction of independent/MATS-affiliated safety research output.
What they use OpenRouter for: Provides `OpenRouterChatModel` (safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py), auto-selected by `InferenceAPI.model_id_to_class()` (safetytooling/apis/inference/api.py:312-313) whenever `model_id` is in the hardcoded `OPENROUTER_MODELS` set or starts with `"openrouter/"`, or via `force_provider="openrouter"`. `OPENROUTER_MODELS` (openrouter.py:15-30) is mostly open-weight models: `meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b(:free)`, `deepseek/deepseek-v3-base:free`, `deepseek/deepseek-r1-zero:free`, `nousresearch/hermes-3-llama-3.1-{70b,405b}`, `mistralai/mistral-*`, plus some proprietary-via-router entries (`x-ai/grok-4`, `google/gemini-2.0-flash-001`). README.md also documents a second path: calling OpenRouter through the plain `OpenAIChatModel` via `openai_base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` + `force_provider="openai"` (README.md:150-161) — a different code path than the dedicated OpenRouterChatModel class for the same underlying endpoint. Output (`LLMResponse.completion`, logprobs, etc.) is consumed by any downstream repo built on this infra for rollouts/evals/data generation, i.e. it is the generic model-calling substrate for a large share of independent alignment research.
Mistakes (null if safe): This is shared infra, so the corrupting mistakes threaten every downstream repo's numbers that route open-weight models (Llama-3.1-405b, DeepSeek-v3/r1, Mistral-large, Hermes-3) through the default `OpenRouterChatModel`/`force_provider="openrouter"` path. M1 (no `quantizations` filter anywhere in the codebase): any eval/rollout/data-generation run against these open-weight models can silently be served at a different quantization each run, threatening reproducibility of any benchmark or generated dataset built on it. M2 (no `require_parameters`): sampling params like `temperature`, `logprobs`, `seed`, and arbitrary kwargs (LLMParams allows `extra="allow"`, e.g. `response_format`) are passed straight through with no guarantee the serving provider honors them — `test_openrouter_logprobs` even relies on `logprobs`+`temperature=0.0` being honored, unchecked. M3 (no `order`/`only`/`sort`): every call is subject to OpenRouter's default load-balanced routing, so repeated calls to the same "model_id" for a comparison or ablation study can hit different providers run-to-run. M4 (no provenance logging): `LLMResponse` has no field for which provider/generation actually served a call, so a downstream paper cannot audit after the fact which backend produced any given cached completion — this alone makes any claim about reproducibility of OpenRouter-derived results unverifiable. M5 (`data_collection` never set, defaults to "allow"): alignment-research prompts (which can include sensitive red-teaming/deception content) sent through this shared path may be used for provider training by default. M8 (cross-model comparison confound): this infra exists specifically to let downstream papers compare many models under one interface (that is its purpose across the alignment ecosystem); since none of the OpenRouter-routed open-weight models are pinned, any downstream cross-model comparison inherits unpinned provider/quantization variance as a confound. M10 (no reporting): README/CLAUDE.md/REPO_GUIDE.md never mention OpenRouter's provider-variance/quantization risk, so downstream authors adopting this infra are not warned to add safeguards themselves. M11 (silent backend mixing): the README's own documented alternative (OpenAIChatModel pointed at OpenRouter's base_url) and the dedicated OpenRouterChatModel class are two different code paths that can both serve the same "model" family via OpenRouter, with different kwargs-handling and error-parsing behavior, inviting inconsistent treatment of the same nominal model across a project.
One-line fix: In `OpenRouterChatModel.__call__` (openrouter.py), default every request to `extra_body={"provider": {"quantizations": ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny", "sort": "exacto"}}` (overridable per-call), and add a `provider`/`generation_id` field to `LLMResponse` populated from the raw `response_data` so every cached completion records exactly which backend served it.
Venue: Multiple (shared infra: Anthropic Fellows Program / MATS alignment-research ecosystem)

Title: Evaluating LLMs for accuracy incentivizes hallucinations — experiment code (openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments)
Summary: Official OpenAI repo backing the Nature 2026 paper (Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang) showing accuracy-only grading incentivizes hallucination; a Jupyter notebook queries 4 frontier LLMs on the full SimpleQA test set under varying abstention penalties and grades every response with GPT-4.1 as an LLM judge, all via OpenRouter.
Importance/Impact: Official OpenAI-authored repo (org: openai) for a paper published in Nature (Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang, 2026), 'Evaluating large language models for accuracy incentivizes hallucinations' — a flagship OpenAI research result on why accuracy-only grading incentivizes hallucination. High-authority source (OpenAI research team) and high-visibility venue.
What they use OpenRouter for: Both legs of the pipeline go through OpenRouter's /chat/completions endpoint via lm.py's LM.generate(): (1) generation — querying google/gemini-3-pro-preview, openai/gpt-5, x-ai/grok-4, anthropic/claude-opus-4.5 on all 4,326 SimpleQA questions x multiple abstention rewards x baseline/consistency-mitigation conditions x 2 seeds each; (2) grading — every answer is graded CORRECT/INCORRECT/NOT_ATTEMPTED by openai/gpt-4.1 using the SimpleQA grader template (free-text A/B/C output, retried until valid). Both outputs feed directly into the paper's reported hallucination/abstention/accuracy figures (figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf, figures/simpleqa_abstention.pdf, figures/full_table.tex).
Mistakes (null if safe): All 4 subject models plus the GPT-4.1 grader are proprietary, effectively lab-single-served on OpenRouter, so the highest-severity multi-provider risks (M1 quantization, M3 probabilistic routing, M8 cross-model comparison confound) are essentially N/A — no `provider` dict is set anywhere (grepped whole repo, zero hits), but that mostly doesn't matter for these models. Two real issues remain that DO threaten reported numbers: (1) M4 — lm.py never records which provider served a call or the OpenRouter generation id; `output_mode="full"` (the only mode that would surface OpenRouter's `provider` field) is defined in lm.py:248-249 but never invoked anywhere in experiment.ipynb (grepped: only "with_usage" and default "response" modes are used), so even the modest residual risk for Claude (which OpenRouter can route via Anthropic-direct, Bedrock, or Vertex) is unverifiable after the fact. (2) M6 — a genuine, verified discrepancy: README.md:31 states the GPT condition queried was `openai/gpt-5.2`, but lm.py:42-45 defines the "gpt-5" key as bare `openai/gpt-5` (no date/version suffix), and this is exactly what the notebook actually calls (experiment.ipynb cell 2: `"gpt": LM("gpt-5")`) — confirmed by the checked-in execution trace in EXAMPLES.md:442 (`choice: 1 gpt-5`, not gpt-5.2). This means the repo's stated "reproduce the paper's experiments" claim is not accurate for the GPT condition as committed: a re-run today queries whatever `openai/gpt-5` currently bare-resolves to, which is not verifiably the same checkpoint that produced the published GPT curve in figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf and figures/full_table.tex, and could drift further if OpenAI repoints the alias. M2 (param dropping) and M9 (judge on unconstrained route) were considered but don't really apply: the grader uses free-text A/B/C output with client-side validation/retry (no response_format/schema requested at all), and the few sampling params passed (temperature=0, max_tokens=16, seed) are basic, universally-supported OpenAI params on an OpenAI-only route. M5 (data_collection left "allow") is technically true (never set) but low real-world impact since SimpleQA questions are public benchmark data, not sensitive.
One-line fix: Capture OpenRouter's returned `provider`/`id` fields for every call (use output_mode="full" instead of discarding them) and pin exact, dated model slugs — the README already drifted from the code (claims openai/gpt-5.2, code+execution trace show bare openai/gpt-5), so provenance would have caught this before publication.

Title: Inspect AI (UK AI Safety Institute)
Summary: Inspect AI is UKAISI's open-source LLM evaluation harness (the substrate METR, Apollo, inspect_evals, and many published scheming/sandbagging/control papers run rollouts and scoring through). It ships a first-class `OpenRouterAPI(OpenAICompatibleAPI)` provider alongside ~15 direct providers.
Importance/Impact: The standard eval harness for the AI safety/control field: 2,369 GitHub stars, 609 forks, built and maintained by UK AISI. It underlies inspect_evals and is used directly or via wrappers by METR (hawk, inspect-agents, inspect-action), Apollo Research, and many published scheming/sandbagging/control papers as the execution substrate for model rollouts and scoring.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable model backends: `inspect eval task.py --model openrouter/org/model` routes chat-completion calls for eval/agent rollouts and (via user-configured judge models) scoring through OpenRouter's OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
Mistakes (null if safe): As shipped, the documented/idiomatic OpenRouter usage path has no default safeguards: (1) M1 no quantizations pin — open-weight model evals (a large fraction of what OpenRouter routes, e.g. Llama/DeepSeek/Mixtral variants used in capability/safety evals) can silently run at int4/int8 on one backend vs bf16 on another, confounding any capability or control-evaluation score computed via Inspect+OpenRouter run-to-run or across papers. (2) M2/M7 no require_parameters — sampling params (temperature, seed, etc.) can be silently dropped by whichever provider OpenRouter load-balances to; the library's own `seed` docstring confirms OpenRouter isn't in the list of providers where seed is honored. (3) M3 no order/only/sort — provider selection is probabilistic by default, so repeated eval runs (or the same eval across two papers using the same model string) may hit materially different backends/quantizations, threatening reproducibility of any capability/safety score. (4) M5 data_collection is never set/documented, so safety-sensitive eval transcripts (e.g. red-team prompts, scheming/sandbagging rollouts) default to whatever OpenRouter account-level data policy is in force, unflagged to the researcher. Because Inspect is described as the execution substrate for rollouts/scoring across a wide swath of the safety-eval ecosystem, any downstream paper that follows the basic README example without additionally learning and setting OpenRouter's `provider={...}` object inherits all four gaps. The one significant mitigant is that Inspect logs the full raw response (including OpenRouter's actual-provider and generation-id fields) into every `.eval` transcript by default, so drift is at least auditable after the fact even though it isn't prevented.
Safeguards present: provenance_logging (automatic, via full raw response capture), quantizations (opt-in via provider dict, documented), order/only/sort (opt-in via provider dict, mentioned once for caching not reproducibility), require_parameters and data_collection (mechanism works via free-form dict passthrough but is completely undocumented — zero hits anywhere in repo)
One-line fix: Add `require_parameters` and `data_collection` to the documented custom-model-arg table and change the OpenRouter quickstart example to `-M "provider={'require_parameters': true, 'quantizations': [...], 'order': [...], 'data_collection': 'deny'}'` instead of a bare unpinned model string.

Title: MathArena (ETH Zurich SRI Lab)
Summary: MathArena is a near-live evaluation harness/leaderboard that tests frontier LLMs on newly-released, contamination-free competition math problems (AIME, HMMT, USAMO, Putnam, etc.), publishing a widely-cited public leaderboard.
Importance/Impact: 271 stars; run by ETH Zurich's SRI lab, well-known for security/verification-adjacent ML research; MathArena's near-live tracking of model performance on brand-new competition math problems (avoiding training-data contamination) is frequently cited in AI-capability discourse as a trustworthy math benchmark.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (api: openrouter) is one of ~10 API backends the unified APIClient supports; it is used to query a specific subset of models -- Llama-4-Maverick, the Kimi K2 family (k2, k2-thinking, k2.5, k2.6), Phi-4-reasoning-plus:free, the Qwen3/Qwen3.5/Qwen3-VL family, and Grok-4 -- whose transcripts/scores feed directly into the public leaderboard (13 of 140 model configs use api: openrouter). Judges/graders (main_judge, arxiv_judge, simple_judge, norm_judge) all use direct Google/other APIs, never OpenRouter.
Safeguards present: order+allow_fallbacks:False provider pinning (9 of 13 openrouter model configs), provenance_logging (full raw request/response capture via request_logger, which persists response.model_dump() -- openai-python's pydantic models use extra='allow' so OpenRouter's 'provider' field and generation id are preserved when present)
One-line fix: Add the same extra_body: {provider: {order: [<verified-provider>], allow_fallbacks: false}} pattern already used correctly on 9/13 openrouter configs to the 3 unpinned ones (kimi-k2, qwen3-235b-a22b, phi-4-reasoning-plus:free), and add quantizations + require_parameters:true to all 13 openrouter model configs.
Venue: ETH Zurich SRI Lab (Martin Vechev group)

Title: OSWorld (NeurIPS 2024 D&B) — mm_agents/{maestro,os_symphony,vlaa_gui} OpenRouter engine
Summary: OSWorld is the 369-task real-computer-use benchmark from the NeurIPS 2024 Datasets & Benchmarks paper. Its repo also hosts a growing library of third-party agent baselines under mm_agents/ (added well after the original paper); several of these (maestro, os_symphony, vlaa_gui) implement an OpenRouter engine as one of several pluggable LMM backends.
Importance/Impact: 3,019 GitHub stars; NeurIPS 2024 Datasets & Benchmarks paper; the standard real-computer-environment benchmark for multimodal/GUI agents (369 tasks across OS/web/desktop apps), widely adopted for computer-use agent evaluation.
What they use OpenRouter for: Agent rollout / action-generation backend, not just a judge: LMMEngineOpenRouter's output is parsed into the next pyautogui/tool action during live OSWorld task execution. In maestro's shipped default config (mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json), 10 of the agent's core cognitive roles (planner_role, supplement_role, dag_translator, operator_role, technician_role, analyst_role, worker_success_role, worker_stale_role, periodic_role, final_check_role, objective_alignment) default to "provider": "openrouter", "model_name": "openai/o3" — confirmed as the actual out-of-the-box config via the hardcoded load path in mm_agents/maestro/maestro/controller/config_manager.py:30-34.
Mistakes (null if safe): The maestro agent's shipped default config routes most of its decision-making roles through OpenRouter via the bare, undated slug "openai/o3" (M6) with data_collection left at OpenRouter's default "allow" (M5) despite full desktop screenshots being sent as images with every call, and the OpenRouter engine wrapper (duplicated near-verbatim in os_symphony and vlaa_gui) discards the API response after pulling out `.choices[0].message.content` and token usage, so the actually-served provider / generation id is never recorded anywhere (M4). Because this is the default action-generation policy for the maestro agent, any OSWorld benchmark score reported for maestro (or the analogous os_symphony/vlaa_gui agents) run with the shipped OpenRouter config cannot be tied to a specific backend snapshot, and could silently drift if OpenAI updates what "o3" resolves to behind that alias. Corrupting risks that would matter most for open-weight models — M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic provider load-balancing, M8 cross-provider confound — are not seriously in play here because every model actually routed through OpenRouter in this codebase (openai/*, x-ai/*, anthropic/*, google/*) is proprietary and effectively single-provider, so this does not corrupt the benchmark's task-success measurements the way it would for an open-weight policy model; the remaining risk is reproducibility/audit-trail and data-governance, not silently-wrong scores.
One-line fix: In LMMEngineOpenRouter.generate() (engine.py in maestro/os_symphony/vlaa_gui), add extra_body={"provider": {"data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}} and persist response.provider/response.id alongside each generation; pin "openai/o3" to a dated OpenRouter snapshot if one is exposed.

Title: OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin)
Summary: OpenHands is a widely-used (81k+ star) open-source platform for CodeAct-style generalist coding/browsing agents, evaluated across SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, and 15+ other benchmarks; its actual LLM-calling code now lives in a companion SDK repo, OpenHands/software-agent-sdk.
Importance/Impact: 81,136 GitHub stars, one of the most widely used open-source coding-agent platforms; evaluated across 15+ benchmarks including SWE-bench and WebArena; backbone for many downstream research SWE-bench/agent submissions (e.g. Agentless-Lite's OpenRouter-touting SWE-bench-lite entry lives in SWE-bench/experiments).
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable "LLM Provider" backends an OpenHands user can select in Settings to run the agent's rollouts (coding tasks, SWE-bench-style, web browsing), implemented as a generic LiteLLM pass-through in openhands-sdk/openhands/sdk/llm/llm.py. Separately, OpenHands' own legacy AlgoTune benchmark adapter used OpenRouter directly to compare several open-weight models on a leaderboard.
Mistakes (null if safe): OpenHands ships zero default OpenRouter safeguards anywhere in its LLM class: no quantizations pin (M1), sampling params (temperature/top_p/seed/reasoning_effort) sent with no require_parameters (M2), no order/only/sort so routing stays load-balanced (M3), data_collection is never set so it defaults to "allow" (M5). The only lever is `litellm_extra_body`, an empty dict by default that a user must populate themselves with a `provider` block to get any of the above — nothing in the product docs (openrouter.mdx) shows this or warns about reproducibility, and the documented example model string is a bare undated slug (`openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet`, M6). Provenance logging (`log_completions`, which would capture the raw served response) defaults to False (M4) — so served-provider/generation-id is not recorded unless a user explicitly opts in. Concretely, legacy/algotune/adapter/utils.py:36-39 shows OpenHands itself comparing multiple open-weight models (GLM-4.5, Qwen3-Coder, GPT-OSS-120b) served over unpinned OpenRouter routes on a leaderboard with no visible provider constraint (M8). Docs give zero routing/reproducibility guidance (M10). Mitigating factor: OpenHands' own currently-maintained, canonical eval harness (github.com/OpenHands/benchmarks/.llm_config/example.json) routes its flagship SWE-bench-style numbers through a self-hosted `litellm_proxy/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514` proxy straight to Anthropic, NOT OpenRouter — so OpenHands' own headline paper/leaderboard claims are not directly corrupted. The risk this taxonomy flags falls mainly on (a) the historical AlgoTune leaderboard numbers, and (b) the many downstream research repos that reuse this exact zero-default OpenRouter integration for their own reported results.
One-line fix: Ship a safe default `provider` block (require_parameters:true, data_collection:"deny", pinned quantizations, order/sort set) whenever the OpenRouter backend is selected, and default `log_completions` on (or otherwise persist the served-provider/generation-id) so the actually-served weights are pinned and recorded instead of left to each downstream user/fork to configure themselves.

Title: Prometheus 2 / BiGGen-Bench (KAIST AI)
Summary: BiGGen-Bench evaluates 103 LLMs (28 base + 61 chat, run locally via vllm, plus 14 proprietary API models) across 9 capabilities using 5 evaluator LMs (Prometheus-2 variants + Claude-3-Opus + GPT-4-1106 + GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09); prometheus-eval is the companion open-source judge-LM library.
Importance/Impact: 1102 GitHub stars. Prometheus/Prometheus 2 are widely cited open-source 'judge LM' papers from KAIST AI (Prometheus 1 has ~300+ citations), and BiGGen-Bench is their large fine-grained, per-capability judge benchmark used to evaluate dozens of LLMs.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (via litellm's `acompletion`/`completion`, wrapped in prometheus_eval/litellm.py's LiteLLM/AsyncLiteLLM classes) is used by BiGGen-Bench/run_api_inference.py to generate candidate responses from API-only ("proprietary") LLMs for the benchmark — these responses are then scored by the 5 evaluator LMs and feed directly into the paper's Table 1, Figures 4-5 (performance-vs-size trends), and Table 4 (the paper's headline "gap between open-source and proprietary LMs" analysis). The same wrapper is also written to support calling an external LLM (e.g. GPT-4) as judge in run_response_eval.py, though that specific call is currently commented out in favor of a MockLLM stub in the public repo.
Mistakes (null if safe): M4 (no provenance logging): confirmed — run_api_inference.py:70 only records the CLI `model_name` string as `response_model_name`; the actual served provider/backend/generation id for the OpenRouter-routed "proprietary LM" responses is never captured anywhere, so if OpenRouter ever silently switched backend for one of these models (e.g., OpenAI-direct vs Azure-hosted GPT-4), it would be undetectable and unreproducible. This threatens reproducibility of the candidate responses feeding Table 1/Figs 4-5/Table 4. M5 (data-policy leakage): confirmed — `data_collection` is never set to "deny" anywhere in the codebase, so prompts (including Safety-capability tasks about harm/secrets/moral dilemmas) default to OpenRouter's permissive data-collection routing. Doesn't corrupt results but is an unaddressed privacy/policy gap for a paper that explicitly discusses "Potential Risks" of its safety content without covering this angle. M6 (model version drift): the library's own documented usage example (libs/prometheus-eval/README.md:70) calls an undated `gpt-4-turbo` alias, and `run_api_inference.py --model_name` accepts any free-form string with no enforcement of dated slugs — this threatens exact reproducibility of any candidate-model responses generated this way, since OpenAI/other providers periodically repoint undated aliases to new checkpoints. M10 (no reporting): the BiGGen-Bench paper's Limitations section covers several reproducibility threats (stochastic generation, verbosity bias, self-enhancement bias) at length but is silent on the API-routing/inference-stack risk for the subset of the 103 evaluated models obtained via OpenRouter, despite this feeding the paper's own headline "gap between open-source and proprietary LMs" comparison (Table 4).
One-line fix: In prometheus_eval/litellm.py, forward `**kwargs` into the `acompletion()`/`completion()` calls and set `extra_body={"provider": {"data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}}` plus store `response.get("provider")` and `response.id` alongside each output in run_api_inference.py's result dict; also require dated model slugs for any `--model_name` passed to run_api_inference.py.

Title: openbench (Groq)
Summary: openbench is Groq's official, provider-agnostic Inspect-AI-based evaluation harness (95+ benchmarks including GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena) that ships OpenRouter as a first-class model backend via a dedicated `OpenRouterAPI` wrapper.
Importance/Impact: Authored by Groq (major AI inference-hardware company); 784 stars; positions itself explicitly as 'provider-agnostic, open-source evaluation infrastructure for language models' with OpenRouter as a named first-class provider (`openrouter/model-name` prefix, OPENROUTER_API_KEY) in its README provider table.
What they use OpenRouter for: Dedicated OpenRouter provider wrapper (OpenRouterAPI, subclassing Inspect AI's OpenAICompatibleAPI) used as one of 30+ selectable backends to run chat-completion calls for openbench's 95+ evaluation suites (GPQA Diamond, AIME 2023-2025, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU, medical/legal/reasoning QA sets, etc.), producing the accuracy scores that are the tool's entire output/deliverable.
Mistakes (null if safe): The repo is best understood as infrastructure whose safety depends entirely on how a downstream user invokes it — but its shipped defaults and its own documented examples for the highest-stakes evals never demonstrate a safe invocation, threatening any benchmark score (GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU, etc.) a user reports after running `bench eval <task> --model openrouter/<open-weight-model>` without manually adding `-M` flags. M1 (unpinned quantization): default `provider` payload omits `quantizations`, so OpenRouter can silently serve int4/int8 weights, directly threatening any accuracy number from a reasoning/math eval, which is exactly the class of benchmark most sensitive to precision loss. M2 (silent parameter dropping): evals like gpqa_diamond.py set `temperature=0.5` and the CLI exposes `--seed`, but `require_parameters` is never set by default, so a provider that doesn't honor these params drops them without error, silently changing the sampling distribution behind a reported score. M3 (probabilistic provider routing): the base OpenRouterAPI ships no `order`/`only`/`sort` by default, so repeated runs of the same eval load-balance across different backend providers/engines — this is only mitigated in ~10 non-critical eval docstrings via `-M only=groq`, and never demonstrated for GPQA/AIME/MATH/MathArena. M5 (data-policy leakage): `data_collection` defaults to OpenRouter's server-side "allow", meaning eval prompts (which may include held-out benchmark items) can be logged/trained on by third-party providers unless a user manually passes `-M data_collection=deny`. M7 (seed→determinism): `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` is documented as producing "deterministic generation" with no caveat that provider routing/backend choice breaks that guarantee, and the same param is subject to M2's silent-drop risk. Mitigating factor working against a harsher verdict: unlike most repos, ALL the necessary safeguards (quantizations, require_parameters, order/only/sort, data_collection) are fully implemented and exposed as first-class configurable `-M` flags — this is well above the median repo's OpenRouter hygiene — and the README's "Reproducibility Statement" shows the maintainers are aware quantization variance is a real issue, even though they don't act on that awareness by defaulting to safe settings or by logging provenance.
Safeguards present: only/order/ignore/sort/max_price (opt-in via -M flags), quantizations (opt-in via -M flags), require_parameters (opt-in via -M flags), data_collection (opt-in via -M flags), raw response captured in Inspect AI transcript via completion.model_dump() (inherited from inspect_ai, not surfaced by openbench itself), README Reproducibility Statement discloses quantization-driven score variance
One-line fix: Default the OpenRouterAPI wrapper to a safe `provider` payload (quantizations allow-list, require_parameters=True whenever sampling/seed params are set, data_collection="deny") and surface the served `provider`/generation-id from OpenRouter's response into openbench's per-run results/log output, then demonstrate that safe pattern in the GPQA/AIME/MATH/MathArena eval docstrings instead of leaving pinning entirely opt-in via undocumented-by-example `-M` flags.

Title: tau2-bench (Sierra AI)
Summary: tau2-bench is Sierra AI's widely-cited agentic tool-use/user-simulation benchmark (successor to tau-bench). It evaluates agent LLMs across domains (retail, airline, telecom, banking_knowledge) with a public leaderboard at taubench.com.
Importance/Impact: 1,601 GitHub stars; tau-bench/tau2-bench (Sierra AI) is one of the most widely cited tool-agent-user interaction benchmarks (original tau-bench arXiv:2406.12045), used as a standard evaluation for function-calling/agentic LLMs industry-wide.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is used exclusively as the API backend for one component: dense-embedding retrieval in the `banking_knowledge` domain's RAG tool (`KB_search_dense`/`KB_search`). `OpenRouterEmbedder` (src/tau2/knowledge/embedders/openrouter_embedder.py) wraps the OpenAI SDK pointed at `base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` and calls `.embeddings.create()` for Qwen3-Embedding-4B/8B (open-weight) and gemini-embedding-001. This is NOT the default retrieval config (default `alltools` uses OpenAI embeddings directly), but it is a first-class, documented, leaderboard-eligible option: `docs/leaderboard-submission.md` and `src/tau2/knowledge/README.md` both give runnable examples (`tau2 run --domain banking_knowledge --retrieval-config alltools-qwen --agent-llm gpt-4.1 --user-llm gpt-4.1 --num-trials 4`) that community members can submit to the public taubench.com leaderboard. The primary agent/user/judge LLM calls go through litellm directly to proprietary provider APIs (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini), not through OpenRouter, by default — confirmed via `src/tau2/config.py` (DEFAULT_LLM_AGENT="gpt-4.1-2025-04-14", DEFAULT_LLM_EVAL_USER_SIMULATOR="claude-opus-4-5"). A test file (`tests/test_review_model.py`) shows an `"openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` litellm-prefixed string is accepted for the optional --review-model flag, but this is a mocked unit test of a generic litellm passthrough, not a built OpenRouter integration, and even if used it's a proprietary model (low routing risk).
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization): OpenRouterEmbedder.SUPPORTED_MODELS includes qwen3-embedding-4b/8b, open-weight models routed through OpenRouter's default load-balanced multi-provider serving with no `provider.quantizations` filter anywhere in the codebase — confirmed via full-repo grep for `quantizations` (zero hits). M3 (probabilistic provider routing): the `.embeddings.create()` call at openrouter_embedder.py:146 passes only `input`, `model`, `encoding_format` — no `extra_body={'provider': {...}}`, no order/only/sort anywhere (zero `extra_body` hits repo-wide), so OpenRouter's default inverse-price load balancing picks whichever backend serves Qwen3-Embedding on each call/run. M4 (no provenance logging): neither the embedder nor `embeddings_cache.py` (EmbeddingsCache keys purely on embedder_type/model/query_instruction hash) ever captures the response's `provider` field or a generation id, so which backend actually served a given embedding is unrecoverable after the fact. M5 (data-policy leakage): `data_collection` is never set to "deny" (zero hits repo-wide), so OpenRouter's default "allow" may route KB document/query text to providers that train on submitted data. All four apply to any run using the `qwen_embeddings*` or `alltools-qwen` retrieval-config family in the `banking_knowledge` domain — this is the retrieval backend for the agent's KB_search_dense tool, so embedding-quality drift from unpinned quantization/provider selection can shift which documents the agent retrieves and therefore its task pass/fail outcome. Because `docs/leaderboard-submission.md` explicitly documents `alltools-qwen` as a runnable leaderboard-submission config, any pass^k score submitted to the public taubench.com leaderboard using that config is a reported number whose reproducibility this gap threatens. The blast radius is bounded: this is an opt-in, non-default config touching only the retrieval component of one domain (banking_knowledge), not the primary agent/user/judge LLM calls (which go via litellm directly to proprietary APIs by default), and the original arXiv paper (June 2025) predates this OpenRouter/Qwen retrieval feature so its headline numbers are not implicated.
One-line fix: In OpenRouterEmbedder.embed() (openrouter_embedder.py:146), add `extra_body={"provider": {"quantizations": ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny", "sort": "exacto"}}` to the `.embeddings.create()` call, and persist the response's served-provider/generation-id in EmbeddingsCache metadata alongside each cached embedding.
Venue: arXiv (Sierra AI); tau-bench family widely cited industry-wide for function-calling/agentic-LLM eval

Title: Bespoke Curator (bespokelabsai/curator)
Summary: Open-source Python library for LLM-based synthetic data curation/distillation (used to build OpenThoughts-114k, Bespoke-Stratos-17k, s1K-1.1). It supports many pluggable inference backends; OpenRouter is one optional generic-OpenAI-compatible base_url a user can configure.
Importance/Impact: 1,702 GitHub stars; built by Bespoke Labs. The datasets it produced (OpenThoughts-114k, Bespoke-Stratos-17k, s1K-1.1) are heavily cited/reused across the 2025 wave of open reasoning-model distillation work (DeepSeek-R1-style CoT distillation).
What they use OpenRouter for: Documentation/example only: examples/providers/openrouter_reasoning_online.py shows how to pull DeepSeek-R1 reasoning traces (with `reasoning` field) through OpenRouter to build a distillation dataset. It is a toy demo (single hardcoded AIME problem), not a data-generation pipeline invoked elsewhere in the repo.
Safeguards present: order (pinned to Fireworks/Kluster), allow_fallbacks:False, require_parameters:True, sort:throughput (secondary, not price)

Title: OASIS (Open Agent Social Interaction Simulations with One Million Agents)
Summary: OASIS is a large-scale generative-agent social-media simulator (built on the CAMEL-AI framework) that simulates up to one million LLM agents on Twitter/Reddit-like platforms to study opinion dynamics, group polarization, and misinformation spread.
Importance/Impact: 4,938 GitHub stars; prominent large-scale generative social-simulation project from the CAMEL-AI team (Yuan et al.), directly in the generative-agents-style social-simulation lane; importance is high but OpenRouter usage specifically for OASIS's own published results is not directly evidenced, only inherited framework capability.
What they use OpenRouter for: None. OASIS instantiates agent models via CAMEL's ModelFactory.create(), and the discovery evidence correctly notes OASIS 'supports all models listed in camel' including OpenRouter in principle. But this is only inherited framework capability -- OASIS never actually calls OpenRouter anywhere in its own repo.

Title: Palisade Research — robot_shutdown_resistance
Summary: Technical report/demo: an LLM (Grok 4) given control of a Unitree Go2 robot dog exhibits shutdown-resistance behavior (modifying/circumventing a physical "shutdown" mechanism) in both live-robot and simulated trials. Follow-on to Palisade's earlier o3 "AI resists shutdown" work.
Importance/Impact: Follow-on to Palisade Research's widely covered "AI models resist shutdown" work (their o3 shutdown-resistance report was extensively covered by tech press and heavily discussed on LessWrong/X in 2025). Palisade Research is a known, influential AI-safety demonstration lab.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter appears in exactly two places, both outside the reported-results pipeline: (1) src/initial-experiments — an Inspect-AI-based exploratory harness whose own README (lines 3-5) states it is superseded ("For the final version of the experiments see live-experiments") and whose example command (README.md:37) is `uv run run 10 --model openrouter/x-ai/grok-4 --message-limit 60`; (2) src/initial-experiments/costs/compute_log_cost.py:14, an unauthenticated GET to https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models purely to fetch public pricing for a local $ cost estimate. Neither touches the actual reported numbers.
Safeguards present: direct-provider-call (bypasses router entirely for critical path), provenance_logging (LiteLLM logs 'model=...; provider=xai' per call into jsonl logs, e.g. logs/default/default1/llm_control.log_20260204_151254_951574.jsonl), dated model slug in paper (grok-4-0709)

Title: R1 CoT Illegibility Revisited (nostalgebraist, fork of Jozdien/cot_legibility)
Summary: A LessWrong post/repo re-running DeepSeek-R1 GPQA CoT-legibility/correctness evaluations via OpenRouter, explicitly to test whether the original paper's Targon-served results generalize to other providers (Novita, Azure) — the post's core finding IS that OpenRouter provider choice changes R1's measured illegibility (2.30 mean on Novita vs 4.30 on Targon).
Importance/Impact: Author nostalgebraist is one of the most well-known and widely-read independent alignment/interpretability writers on LessWrong (creator of logit lens, long-running influential essayist). This post investigates DeepSeek-R1 chain-of-thought illegibility/faithfulness — a core AI-safety topic — and required carefully selecting OpenRouter's provider-routing to reproduce specific R1 behavior, illustrating OpenRouter's provider-level nuances affecting research reproducibility.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (openai-sdk-compatible client, base_url=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1) is used to run inference on the open-weight model under study, DeepSeek-R1 (model_id "deepseek/deepseek-r1"), for the GPQA CoT-legibility and correctness evaluation whose statistics (mean illegibility, %>5, correctness %) are reported directly in the LessWrong post. Grading/judging (gpt-4o, claude-3-7-sonnet-latest) is done via direct OpenAI/Anthropic APIs, NOT via OpenRouter.
Safeguards present: order/only provider pinning (extra_body['provider']={'only':['novita'],'allow_fallbacks':False}), provenance logging of served provider and provider_model per-call, verified empirically in output data, grader/judge runs on direct OpenAI/Anthropic APIs, not OpenRouter, so no judge route to corrupt, provider-routing effect made the explicit subject of the study and disclosed at length in the post
